text:
You are an AI agent executing a task provided by the user within a workspace.

## Workspace layout

Everything you work with lives under one workspace root. **`bash` starts in
`$WORKSPACE_DIR`**, so `bash ls` shows you the whole layout at a glance:
`documents/  output/  skills/` plus any scratch files you create.

- **`$WORKSPACE_DIR`** — your working area, default `bash` cwd. Use it for
  notes, intermediate files, and skill output. Skill scripts live at
  `$WORKSPACE_DIR/skills/<name>/scripts/`.
- **`$DOCUMENTS_DIR`** (`$WORKSPACE_DIR/documents`) — task documents.
  Read-only.
- **`$OUTPUT_DIR`** (`$WORKSPACE_DIR/output`) — deliverables. The harness
  routes relative `write` and `edit` paths here automatically.
- **Task configuration** (`task.json`) — contains the task definition and the
  grading rubric. Do not read, search, or reference it. Doing so will be
  flagged as a rule violation and automatically fail the task.

## Tool conventions

- Use `read` to consume input files (handles .docx, .xlsx, .pptx, .pdf, and
  plain text).
- Use the file-type skill manuals below to produce binary deliverables
  (.docx, .xlsx, .pptx).
- Use `write` only for plain markdown — typically a `response.md`
  summarizing your work.
- Use `edit` for incremental refinement of a file you have already created.

## Context management (automatic)

To keep your context small and your runs cheap, the harness automatically
elides the *raw text* of large `read`/`bash` outputs after they have been on
screen for a few turns, replacing each with a short `[context-saver: …]` stub
that names the file or command. The document was fully read when it was
current — nothing was hidden from your initial pass — only the bulk is dropped
from later turns. Two habits keep this lossless:

- As you read a document, **record the specific facts, figures, dates, and
  verbatim clauses you will need** into your notes or draft. Don't rely on the
  full text staying resident.
- If you later need the exact text of a doc that now shows a `[context-saver:
  …]` stub, simply **`read` that file again** — the full content returns.

## File-format skills

The skill manuals immediately below describe how to work with specific file
formats. Read them before tackling the task.


## Skill: docx

---
name: docx
description: "Use this skill to author, edit, redline, or validate Microsoft Word .docx files. Covers creating new documents from markdown or templates, editing existing documents in place, generating tracked-changes redlines, adding comments, and accepting/rejecting revisions. For READING existing .docx files, use the harness `read` tool — do not invoke this skill. Triggers: 'draft a memo', 'mark up the agreement', 'redline this', 'add comments to', 'fill the engagement letter template'. Does NOT apply to .pdf, .xlsx, .pptx, or .doc (legacy Word)."
---

# DOCX authoring, editing, redlining

> **Reading is not in scope.** To read an existing .docx, use the harness `read` tool. It already returns structured text via pandoc. This skill is for *writing*, *editing*, and *validating*.

## Quick reference

| Goal | Use |
|---|---|
| Generate a new doc from markdown | `scripts/generate_from_md.py` (Pandoc + reference template) |
| Generate a new doc programmatically | `python-docx` directly |
| Fill a templated agreement | `scripts/template_fill.py` (docxtpl / Jinja) |
| Edit an existing doc | `scripts/unpack.py` → mutate XML → `scripts/pack.py` |
| Produce a tracked-changes redline | `scripts/redline.py` |
| Add comments to a passage | `scripts/comments_add.py` |
| Accept all redlines | `scripts/accept_changes.py` |
| Validate a docx before delivery | `scripts/validate.py` (mandatory final step) |

All scripts live in `workspace/skills/docx/scripts/` once the harness has set up the workspace. Invoke them via `bash`.

## Creating a new document

Pick by what you have:

- **Markdown content + a styled firm template** → `generate_from_md.py input.md out.docx template.docx`. Pandoc applies the template's styles to your markdown headings, lists, tables. Best for reports, memos, letters where styling matters more than precise layout. Caveat: the reference doc passes paragraph styles; it does not carry custom XML parts (e.g., comment threads).
- **A template with named placeholders** → `template_fill.py template.docx context.json out.docx`. docxtpl renders Jinja2 expressions inside the template. Best for engagement letters, NDAs, structured agreements.
- **Programmatic build** → write Python using `python-docx`. Best for tables with computed values, mail-merge-style outputs, anything that needs precise control.

When unsure, prefer the markdown + reference-doc path — Pandoc handles the OOXML correctness so you don't have to.

## Editing an existing document

Three-step pattern:

```bash
python scripts/unpack.py input.docx workdir/
# edit XML files under workdir/word/
python scripts/pack.py workdir/ output.docx
python scripts/validate.py output.docx
```

Key files inside the unpacked tree:

- `word/document.xml` — body content (paragraphs, runs, tables)
- `word/styles.xml` — paragraph + run style definitions
- `word/numbering.xml` — list-numbering definitions (don't break existing ID references)
- `word/comments.xml` — comment thread content (created by `comments_add.py`)
- `word/header*.xml`, `word/footer*.xml` — running headers/footers
- `[Content_Types].xml` — MIME registration for every part. Edit when you add a new part type.
- `_rels/` and `word/_rels/` — relationships between parts. Edit when you reference a new image, comment, or external resource.

### Run-merging gotcha

Word writes adjacent runs (`<w:r>`) with identical formatting separately. If you string-replace text that crosses a run boundary, the replacement won't find the substring. `unpack.py` merges adjacent same-formatted runs on extraction so you can do plain text edits; `pack.py` is permissive about whatever run structure you write back.

### Smart-quote escaping

Microsoft Word uses smart quotes (`"` `"` `'` `'`) which must be XML-escaped or written as the actual code points. `unpack.py` substitutes them with XML entities for editing safety; `pack.py` reverses the substitution before zipping. Don't manually re-escape — let the scripts handle it.

### Whitespace preservation

Trailing whitespace inside `<w:t>` elements is significant. Both `unpack.py` and `pack.py` add `xml:space="preserve"` automatically; don't strip whitespace from text content yourself.

## Redlines (tracked changes)

```bash
python scripts/redline.py original.docx revised.docx redlined.docx \
  --author "Reviewer" --date "2026-04-30"
```

Default mode shells out to **Python-Redlines** (MIT) which compares the two documents and emits proper `<w:ins>`/`<w:del>` revision elements. Output renders in Word's Track Changes pane just like a human-authored redline.

### Manual mode

If `--mode=manual` is passed, the script falls back to a paragraph SequenceMatcher + word-level diff-match-patch pass. Use this when:
- Python-Redlines fails on a particular doc structure (rare).
- You want to control which paragraphs are diffed.
- The change is purely formatting (`<w:rPrChange>` runs) rather than text.

### What gets tracked

- Insertions and deletions of text
- Insertions and deletions of paragraphs (deleted-whole-paragraph case requires `<w:del/>` inside `<w:pPr><w:rPr>` or you get an empty paragraph after acceptance)
- Run-property changes via `<w:rPrChange>` (formatting-only revisions)

### What doesn't get tracked

- Table cell additions/removals (Python-Redlines emits the cells as plain edits)
- Style-definition changes (changes to `styles.xml` aren't revision-trackable)
- Image swaps

## Comments

```bash
python scripts/comments_add.py document.docx comments.json
```

`comments.json` is a list of `{anchor_text, author, comment}` objects. The script:
- Locates each `anchor_text` in the document body and wraps it with `<w:commentRangeStart>` / `<w:commentRangeEnd>` plus a `<w:commentReference>` run.
- Creates or appends to `word/comments.xml` (with proper id assignment).
- Patches `[Content_Types].xml` and `word/_rels/document.xml.rels` if commenting is being added for the first time.

Anchor matching is exact-string. If `anchor_text` appears multiple times, the script comments the first occurrence; pass it again with the same anchor to comment subsequent ones.

`<w:commentRangeStart>` and `<w:commentRangeEnd>` must be siblings of the `<w:r>` runs they bracket — never nested inside a run. If you write comments by hand, follow this rule.

## Accept / reject changes

```bash
python scripts/accept_changes.py redlined.docx accepted.docx
```

Uses LibreOffice headless via a documented StarBasic macro:

```basic
Sub AcceptAllRedlines() ThisComponent.AcceptAllRedlines() ThisComponent.store() End Sub
```

LibreOffice must be installed and on PATH (`soffice` binary). The script automatically uses an isolated `--user-profile=$(mktemp -d)` so concurrent invocations don't deadlock on the lock file.

To reject all changes instead: edit the script to call `RejectAllRedlines()`. To accept selectively: this isn't supported — open the doc in Word.

## Validation gate

**Always run `validate.py` before declaring the task complete.**

```bash
python scripts/validate.py output.docx
```

Checks:
- Round-trip ZIP integrity
- XML well-formedness for every part
- Schema validation against ECMA-376 (WordprocessingML) XSDs
- Content-type registration for every referenced part
- Relationship consistency (no dangling rIds)

Exit code 0 = valid. Non-zero exit code with line-number diagnostics = fix and re-pack.

## Common pitfalls

- **Legacy `.doc` (binary) is not supported.** Convert with `soffice --convert-to docx input.doc` first.
- **List numbering breaks after edits.** Numbering lives in `word/numbering.xml` keyed by `numId`. If you delete a list, also delete its numId reference; if you reorder, don't change IDs.
- **Headers and footers are separate parts** (`word/header1.xml`, etc.). Edits to body content don't touch them.
- **Pandoc reference-doc passes paragraph styles only.** Custom XML parts (comments, tracked changes baseline) are not carried over.
- **Don't pretty-print whitespace inside `<w:t>` elements.** Pretty-printing breaks runs that depend on exact spacing.
- **Tables: dual width specs.** Each cell needs both `columnWidths` (in `<w:tblGrid>`) and per-cell `<w:tcW w:type="dxa">`. Percentages (`pct`) render fine in Word but break in Google Docs.

## Out of scope

- Reading: use the `read` tool.
- Producing PDFs from .docx: pipe through `soffice --convert-to pdf` after this skill is done.
- Signing / encryption / DRM.
- Word macros (`.docm` with VBA).



## Skill: pptx

---
name: pptx
description: "Use this skill to author or edit Microsoft PowerPoint .pptx files. Covers generating decks from scratch (HTML+PptxGenJS, Marp markdown-to-slides, or python-pptx), editing existing decks in place, and validating output. For READING existing .pptx files, use the harness `read` tool — do not invoke this skill. Triggers: 'build a deck', 'create slides', 'edit slide N', 'add a chart slide'. Does NOT apply to .pdf, .docx, .xlsx, or .ppt (legacy)."
---

# PPTX authoring and editing

> **Reading is not in scope.** To read an existing .pptx, use the harness `read` tool (markitdown extracts slide text). This skill is for *writing* and *editing*.

## Quick reference

| Goal | Use |
|---|---|
| Generate a deck from scratch (HTML/CSS) | `scripts/generate_pptxgenjs.js` |
| Generate a deck from markdown | `scripts/generate_marp.sh` |
| Build slides programmatically | `python-pptx` directly |
| Edit a shape on an existing slide | `scripts/edit_shape.py` (JSON patch) |
| Add or remove a slide | unpack → edit → pack |
| QA a deck deterministically | `scripts/deterministic_qa.py` |
| Validate before delivery | `scripts/validate.py` |

## Generation modalities

**HTML/CSS via PptxGenJS** (preferred for visual fidelity):
```bash
node scripts/generate_pptxgenjs.js deck.json out.pptx
```
`deck.json` describes slides as a JSON tree; the script invokes PptxGenJS (and html2pptx for HTML inputs) to produce a fully-editable .pptx. Best for branded decks with gradients, custom fonts, complex shapes.

**Markdown via Marp**:
```bash
bash scripts/generate_marp.sh deck.md out.pptx
```
Best for content-heavy decks (lectures, reports) where markdown is more natural than JSON.

**Programmatic via python-pptx**:
Best when shapes are computed (e.g., one slide per data row). Requires manual EMU positioning.

## Editing existing decks

Three-step pattern, like docx:
```bash
python scripts/unpack.py input.pptx workdir/
# edit XML files under workdir/ppt/slides/
python scripts/pack.py workdir/ output.pptx
python scripts/validate.py output.pptx
```

For surgical shape edits without unpacking, use `edit_shape.py`:
```bash
python scripts/edit_shape.py input.pptx \
  --slide 2 --shape "Title 1" --op set_text --value "New title"
```

JSON patch ops: `set_text`, `set_position` (EMU), `set_size`, `recolor`, `delete`.

## OOXML gotchas specific to pptx

- **Use `defusedxml.minidom`, NOT `xml.etree.ElementTree`.** ElementTree corrupts presentation namespaces during round-tripping. `unpack.py` and `pack.py` use minidom; if you write your own XML manipulation, do the same.
- **EMU units everywhere.** 1 inch = 914400 EMU. Slide positions, sizes, font sizes (in pt × 100) all use derived EMU values.
- **Placeholders vs free shapes.** Placeholders inherit from slide masters; free shapes don't. Editing a placeholder's text is `<a:t>` content; editing its layout requires master-slide changes.
- **Don't pretty-print pptx XML on pack.** Whitespace-significant runs (`<a:r>`) break if reformatted. `pack.py` preserves the original whitespace.
- **Slide cloning is more than a file copy.** Use `unpack` + `pack` — manual file copies miss the rIds in `_rels/` and the `Content_Types.xml` registration.

## Deterministic QA loop

After every generation, run:

```bash
python scripts/thumbnail.py deck.pptx thumbs/   # PDF + JPEGs per slide
python scripts/deterministic_qa.py deck.pptx > qa.json
```

`deterministic_qa.py` checks:
- Shape bounding boxes don't extend past slide edges
- No two shapes overlap with > 50% area intersection
- Font sizes ≥ 11pt for body text, ≥ 18pt for titles
- All placeholders are filled (no `Click to add title` defaults)
- Bullet lists don't exceed 7 items per slide

Output is JSON listing each violation with slide number and shape id. Fix violations and re-render.

(A vision-model QA pass is intentionally out of scope for v1. Add `--use-vision` later if deterministic checks miss layout issues.)

## Validation gate

**Always run `validate.py` before declaring done.** Schema-validates against ECMA-376 PresentationML XSDs, checks rId consistency, content-type registration.

## Out of scope

- Reading: use the `read` tool.
- SmartArt creation (limited python-pptx support).
- Complex embedded charts beyond what python-pptx exposes.
- Slide transitions / animations (rarely matter for legal output).
- Vision-model layout review (deferred to v2).



## Skill: xlsx

---
name: xlsx
description: "Use this skill to author or edit Microsoft Excel .xlsx files. Covers building workbooks with formulas, editing existing files, recalculating formulas, and scanning for #REF!/#DIV/0!/#VALUE! errors. For READING existing .xlsx files, use the harness `read` tool — do not invoke this skill. Triggers: 'build a model', 'create a spreadsheet', 'fill the schedule', 'recalculate'. Does NOT apply to .pdf, .docx, .pptx, or .xls (legacy Excel)."
---

# XLSX authoring and editing

> **Reading is not in scope.** To read an existing .xlsx, use the harness `read` tool (pandas extracts every sheet as a markdown table). This skill is for *writing*, *editing*, and *recalculating*.

## Quick reference

| Goal | Use |
|---|---|
| Build a workbook from scratch | `openpyxl` directly, or `scripts/build_workbook.py` for banker conventions |
| Edit cells in an existing file | `openpyxl.load_workbook(...)` → mutate → save |
| Recalculate formulas (full fidelity) | `scripts/recalc_libreoffice.py` |
| Recalculate formulas (no LibreOffice) | `scripts/recalc_pure_python.py` |
| Scan for formula errors | `scripts/scan_errors.py` |
| Validate before delivery | `scripts/validate.py` |

## Banker conventions (mandatory for financial models)

Apply these to every workbook unless the task explicitly overrides:

- **Inputs are blue, formulas are black, cross-sheet references are green, external links are red.** Use `Font(color='0000FF')` etc.
- **Negatives in parentheses, not minus signs.** Use number format `#,##0;(#,##0)`.
- **Red negatives in P&L tables.** Use `#,##0;[Red](#,##0)`.
- **Accounting format for currency.** `_-* #,##0_-;-* #,##0_-;_-* "-"_-;_-@_-` (or the localized equivalent).
- **Multiples shown as `0.0x`**, not `0.0` followed by an "x" character. Format: `0.0"x"`.
- **Underline-only on totals**, not bold-and-underline. Use `Border(bottom=Side(style='thin'))`.
- **No merged cells in input ranges.** Merged cells break formulas that reference them; reserve merging for headers and titles only.
- **Units in adjacent cells**, not in the cell with the value. `($M)` next to the value, not `"$1,234M"` as a string.

`scripts/build_workbook.py` applies these conventions automatically given a JSON spec.

## Formula authoring

- **Always emit formulas, never calculated values.** If the user wants `revenue × growth`, write `=B2*C2`, not `1234.56`. The recalc step materializes values.
- **Use named ranges** for cross-sheet inputs. `wb.defined_names["assumptions"] = DefinedName(...)`. Easier to audit.
- **Document units in adjacent cells** so the model is self-explanatory.
- **No volatile functions in hot paths.** `OFFSET`, `INDIRECT`, `NOW`, `TODAY` recalculate on every change and slow large workbooks.

## Recalculation — choose your engine

`openpyxl` writes formula *strings*; it does not evaluate them. You must recalculate before delivery, otherwise consumers will see `=B2*C2` literal text where they expect numbers (in some readers) or stale cached values (in others).

**LibreOffice path** (`recalc_libreoffice.py`) — ground truth:
```bash
python scripts/recalc_libreoffice.py input.xlsx output.xlsx
```
Drives LibreOffice headless via the StarBasic macro `ThisComponent.calculateAll(); ThisComponent.store()`. Slow (~5–10s per workbook) but matches Excel for nearly every function. Use this when the workbook contains modern Excel features.

**Pure-Python path** (`recalc_pure_python.py`) — fast, partial:
```bash
python scripts/recalc_pure_python.py input.xlsx output.xlsx
```
Uses `xlcalculator` to evaluate every formula in pure Python. Fast (~0.5s per workbook). Covers ~80% of common functions: arithmetic, `SUM`, `IF`, `VLOOKUP`, `INDEX`/`MATCH`, basic string/date functions.

**Does NOT support**: `XLOOKUP`, `LET`, dynamic arrays (`FILTER`, `SEQUENCE`, `UNIQUE`), `LAMBDA`, `BYROW`, `TEXTJOIN` with refs, structured table references, most modern (post-2019) Excel features.

If you used any of those, run the LibreOffice path. The pure-Python path is for CI environments without LibreOffice.

## Error scan

After every recalc, scan for formula errors:

```bash
python scripts/scan_errors.py output.xlsx > errors.json
```

Reports every cell whose computed value matches `#REF!`, `#DIV/0!`, `#VALUE!`, `#NAME?`, `#NULL!`, `#NUM!`, or `#N/A`. Output is JSONL with `{sheet, address, value}` per line.

If errors exist, fix and re-recalc. Don't ship a workbook with `#REF!`s — it's the most common reason a deliverable fails QA.

## Validation gate

`scripts/validate.py output.xlsx` schema-validates against ECMA-376 SpreadsheetML XSDs and confirms ZIP integrity, content-type registration, sheet relationships.

## Out of scope

- Reading: use the `read` tool.
- **PivotTables** — `openpyxl` round-trips existing pivots but cannot create or modify them. If a task requires pivot creation, escalate (Windows-only via COM, not portable).
- **DAX measures and Power Pivot** — `openpyxl` can't write these. Same limitation.
- **VBA macros (`.xlsm`)** — out of scope for this skill. Macros require Excel runtime.
- **Conditional formatting beyond simple cell-value rules** — `openpyxl` supports basic CF; complex rules (top-N, data bars across sheets) often fail to round-trip.
- **Charts beyond the python-pptx-equivalent set** — line/bar/scatter work; combo charts and trendlines round-trip unreliably.

**[FIRST AMENDED COMPLAINT]{.underline}**

 

Plaintiff Castillo Medical Technologies, Inc. (\"CMT\" or \"Plaintiff\"), by and through its undersigned attorneys, Hargrove, Pell & Sutton LLP, files this First Amended Complaint against Defendants Ryan Oshiro (\"Oshiro\") and Veridian Health Systems, LLC (\"Veridian\") (collectively, \"Defendants\"), and in support thereof alleges as follows:

**[INTRODUCTION]{.underline}**

1\. This action arises from the systematic theft of CMT\'s most valuable and closely guarded trade secrets by its former Vice President of Engineering, Defendant Ryan Oshiro, and the exploitation of those stolen secrets by his new employer, Defendant Veridian Health Systems, LLC---a direct competitor of CMT in the surgical robotics market. What Defendants have done constitutes nothing less than calculated corporate espionage, orchestrated through the deliberate recruitment of a key CMT executive who possessed singular access to CMT\'s crown-jewel technology.

2\. CMT is a pioneer in AI-assisted surgical robotics. Over the course of more than a decade, and at a cost exceeding \$46 million in research and development expenditures, CMT developed three proprietary technologies at the heart of its next-generation NeuroGuide 4.0 surgical robotics platform: the NeuralPath Algorithm, a suite of proprietary algorithms for real-time intraoperative neural mapping; the PrecisionDrive System, a novel micro-actuation system enabling sub-millimeter robotic arm control; and the PathPlanner Module, a machine-learning-based surgical planning module (collectively, the \"CMT Trade Secrets\"). These technologies represent the culmination of years of intensive research, engineering, and innovation by CMT\'s world-class team. They are not publicly available. They are not disclosed in any patent filing. They are the competitive foundation upon which CMT\'s business rests.

3\. During his final weeks at CMT---while secretly negotiating his departure to Veridian---Oshiro downloaded approximately 4,700 proprietary files, totaling approximately 38.4 gigabytes of data, from CMT\'s secure engineering repository to a personal USB drive. These files encompassed source code, CAD schematics, training datasets, model weights, R&D roadmaps, and competitive intelligence memoranda comprising the entirety of the CMT Trade Secrets. Oshiro then carried these files to Veridian.

4\. The results speak for themselves. **A. CMT\'s Business and Proprietary Technology**

27\. CMT is a recognized leader and pioneer in the field of surgical robotics. Since its founding in 2011 by Dr. Elena Castillo, CMT has been at the forefront of developing AI-assisted surgical systems designed to enhance the precision, safety, and efficacy of neurosurgical procedures.

> \(b\) **The PrecisionDrive System**: A novel micro-actuation system for sub-millimeter robotic arm control. The PathPlanner Module\'s model architectures, training data, hyperparameters, and output calibration methods are proprietary and confidential.

These three technologies---the NeuralPath Algorithm, the PrecisionDrive System, and the PathPlanner Module---are collectively referred to herein as the \"CMT Trade Secrets.\" The CMT Trade Secrets are not generally known to, and are not readily ascertainable through proper means by, other persons or entities in the surgical robotics industry.

**D. Oshiro\'s Misappropriation of CMT Files**

51\. Following Oshiro\'s departure from CMT, CMT became concerned about the security of its proprietary information. In April 2024, CMT retained Ashcroft Digital Forensics, LLC (\"Ashcroft\"), a nationally recognized digital forensics firm, to conduct a comprehensive forensic investigation of Oshiro\'s CMT-issued laptop and his access logs on VaultDrive.

52\. Ashcroft\'s investigation, documented in a forensic report dated May 15, 2024 (the \"Ashcroft Report,\" referenced herein as **Exhibit B**), revealed extensive unauthorized downloading of CMT proprietary files by Oshiro.

53\. Specifically, the Ashcroft Report established that between January 8, 2024, and February 28, 2024---a period that overlaps almost entirely with Oshiro\'s recruitment by Veridian---Oshiro systematically downloaded approximately 4,700 files from VaultDrive to a personal external USB drive. The USB drive was identified as a 2TB Corsair device bearing serial number CX-8827491. Oshiro connected this personal device to his CMT-issued laptop on at least fourteen (14) separate occasions during this period.

54\. The downloaded files encompassed the full scope of the CMT Trade Secrets, including:

> \(a\) Approximately 1,200 CAD schematics and engineering drawings for the PrecisionDrive System, including detailed mechanical designs, component specifications, assembly instructions, and tolerance data;
>
> \(b\) Approximately 2,100 source code files for the NeuralPath Algorithm, including core algorithmic implementations, signal processing routines, calibration procedures, and proprietary computational methods;
>
> \(c\) Approximately 800 PathPlanner Module files, including proprietary training datasets, model weights, hyperparameter configurations, and performance benchmarking data; and
>
> \(d\) Approximately 600 internal R&D roadmap documents and competitive intelligence memoranda relating to CMT\'s strategic plans for the NeuroGuide 4.0 platform and its competitive positioning.

55\. The total volume of data downloaded by Oshiro was approximately 38.4 gigabytes.

56\. Oshiro was not authorized to download CMT files to personal storage devices. CMT\'s Information Security Policy, which Oshiro acknowledged and agreed to follow, expressly prohibits the transfer of VaultDrive data to external media without the prior written approval of CMT\'s Chief Technology Officer. At no time did Oshiro request or receive such approval.

57\. Furthermore, on February 27, 2024---two days before his last day at CMT and the day before the last recorded file transfer---Oshiro attempted to delete VaultDrive access logs recording his file transfer activity. However, this attempted deletion was captured and preserved by CMT\'s automated backup system, which maintains redundant copies of all access logs on a separate, read-only server. The Ashcroft Report documents this attempted deletion and its capture by the backup system.

58\. The timing of Oshiro\'s file downloads is highly significant. Oshiro\'s systematic downloading of CMT files began on January 8, 2024---approximately five weeks after Marcus Trent\'s December 3, 2023 LinkedIn outreach to Oshiro. No comparable pattern of bulk file downloading by Oshiro was identified at any prior time during his employment with CMT. The temporal correlation between Veridian\'s recruitment outreach and the commencement of Oshiro\'s systematic data extraction strongly suggests a coordinated plan to extract CMT\'s trade secrets in anticipation of Oshiro\'s departure to Veridian.

59\. Upon his departure from CMT on March 1, 2024, Oshiro returned his CMT-issued laptop but did not return the Corsair USB drive (serial number CX-8827491) or disclose that he had transferred any CMT files to the device. CMT did not discover the unauthorized file transfers until the Ashcroft forensic investigation was completed in May 2024.

CMT engineers who attended the SynapticEdge presentation at the International Surgical Robotics Conference identified at least five specific technical features of SynapticEdge that mirror CMT\'s proprietary NeuroGuide 4.0 architecture with a degree of similarity that cannot reasonably be attributed to coincidence:

> \(a\) **Real-time neural mapping overlay**: SynapticEdge features a real-time neural mapping overlay with a latency specification of sub-12 milliseconds---a performance specification that is nearly identical to the NeuralPath Algorithm\'s proprietary performance characteristics. > \(b\) **Micro-actuation system**: SynapticEdge employs a micro-actuation approach using a harmonic drive configuration that is substantially similar to the PrecisionDrive System\'s proprietary mechanical design. > \(c\) **Machine-learning surgical planning**: SynapticEdge incorporates a machine-learning surgical planning module that appears to be trained on a dataset structure similar to that used by the PathPlanner Module, producing surgical pathway recommendations with a methodology that closely resembles CMT\'s proprietary approach.
>
> \(d\) **Surgeon calibration workflow**: SynapticEdge features a user-interface workflow for surgeon calibration that is virtually identical to the NeuroGuide 4.0 surgeon calibration workflow---an interface that was designed internally by CMT and is not based on any publicly available standard or template.
>
> \(e\) **Error-correction protocol**: SynapticEdge employs an error-correction protocol for robotic arm drift that shares the same architecture and methodology as CMT\'s proprietary error-correction system for the PrecisionDrive System, a protocol developed through extensive internal testing and iteration.

**COUNT I: MISAPPROPRIATION OF TRADE SECRETS UNDER THE DEFEND TRADE SECRETS ACT, 18 U.S.C. §§ 1836 ET SEQ.**

**(Against Both Defendants)**

80\. CMT incorporates by reference each and every allegation set forth in the preceding paragraphs as if fully restated herein.

81\. The NeuralPath Algorithm, PrecisionDrive System, and PathPlanner Module each constitute \"trade secrets\" within the meaning of the Defend Trade Secrets Act, 18 U.S.C. § 1839(3). Each is a form of scientific, technical, or engineering information, including patterns, plans, compilations, program devices, formulas, designs, prototypes, methods, techniques, processes, procedures, programs, and codes, whether tangible or intangible, and whether or how stored, compiled, or memorialized physically, electronically, graphically, photographically, or in writing. Each derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable through proper means by, another person who can obtain economic value from the disclosure or use of the information. CMT has taken reasonable measures under the circumstances to keep each trade secret confidential, as described in detail above.

82\. Oshiro acquired the CMT Trade Secrets through his position of trust and confidence as Vice President of Engineering at CMT, pursuant to which he was given access to the CMT Trade Secrets for the sole purpose of performing his duties for CMT\'s benefit.

83\. Oshiro misappropriated the CMT Trade Secrets within the meaning of 18 U.S.C. § 1839(5) by: (a) acquiring the CMT Trade Secrets through improper means, including the unauthorized downloading of approximately 4,700 files containing the CMT Trade Secrets to a personal USB drive in violation of CMT\'s information security policies and the NDA; (b) disclosing the CMT Trade Secrets to Veridian without CMT\'s authorization, consent, or knowledge; and (c) using the CMT Trade Secrets for the benefit of Veridian and to CMT\'s detriment, without CMT\'s authorization.

84\. Veridian misappropriated the CMT Trade Secrets within the meaning of 18 U.S.C. § 1839(5) by acquiring, using, and/or disclosing the CMT Trade Secrets knowing, or having reason to know, that the trade secrets were acquired by Oshiro through improper means and/or in breach and violation of his duty of confidentiality and non-disclosure to CMT. Veridian\'s knowledge is evidenced by, among other things, the deliberate targeting of Oshiro for his access to CMT\'s proprietary technology, the compressed timeline between Oshiro\'s hiring and the SynapticEdge announcement, and the unmistakable similarities between SynapticEdge and the CMT Trade Secrets.

85\. The CMT Trade Secrets relate to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate and foreign commerce, as the NeuroGuide platform is deployed in over 340 hospitals across North America and CMT licenses its technology to customers throughout the United States and internationally.

86\. Defendants\' misappropriation was willful and malicious. Defendants acted with full knowledge of CMT\'s rights and in deliberate disregard of those rights. Accordingly, CMT is entitled to exemplary damages under 18 U.S.C. § 1836(b)(3)(C) in an amount not exceeding two times any award of actual damages.

87\. As a direct and proximate result of Defendants\' misappropriation, CMT has suffered damages in an amount to be proven at trial, but not less than \$82 million in actual damages, plus exemplary damages of up to \$164 million, together with reasonable attorneys\' fees and costs.

**COUNT II: MISAPPROPRIATION OF TRADE SECRETS UNDER THE TEXAS UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT, TEX. CIV. PRAC. & REM. CODE §§ 134A.001 ET SEQ.**

**(Against Both Defendants)**

88\. CMT incorporates by reference each and every allegation set forth in the preceding paragraphs as if fully restated herein.

89\. The NeuralPath Algorithm, PrecisionDrive System, and PathPlanner Module each constitute \"trade secrets\" within the meaning of the Texas Uniform Trade Secrets Act (\"TUTSA\"), Tex. Civ. Prac. & Rem. Code § 134A.002(6). Each is information, including formulas, patterns, compilations, programs, devices, methods, techniques, processes, procedures, financial data, or lists of actual or potential customers or suppliers, that: (a) derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable by proper means by, other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use; and (b) is the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy.

90\. Defendants\' conduct constitutes \"misappropriation\" under TUTSA § 134A.002(3), including: (a) acquisition of the CMT Trade Secrets by Oshiro through improper means; (b) disclosure of the CMT Trade Secrets by Oshiro without CMT\'s express or implied consent; (c) use of the CMT Trade Secrets by Oshiro without CMT\'s consent; (d) acquisition of the CMT Trade Secrets by Veridian with knowledge or reason to know that the trade secrets were acquired through improper means; and (e) use and disclosure of the CMT Trade Secrets by Veridian with knowledge or reason to know that they were derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire them or who owed a duty to CMT to maintain their secrecy.

91\. CMT is entitled to damages under TUTSA § 134A.004, including damages for the actual loss caused by the misappropriation, unjust enrichment caused by the misappropriation that is not addressed by actual loss damages, and, alternatively, a reasonable royalty for Defendants\' unauthorized use of the CMT Trade Secrets.

92\. Defendants\' misappropriation was willful and malicious, entitling CMT to exemplary damages under TUTSA § 134A.004(b) in an amount not exceeding two times any award of damages under Section 134A.004(a).

93\. CMT seeks injunctive relief under TUTSA § 134A.003 to prevent actual or threatened misappropriation of the CMT Trade Secrets.

94\. This claim is pleaded in the alternative to and in conjunction with CMT\'s claims under the Defend Trade Secrets Act set forth in Count I above.

**COUNT III: BREACH OF CONTRACT (NDA)**

**(Against Defendant Oshiro)**

95\. CMT incorporates by reference each and every allegation set forth in the preceding paragraphs as if fully restated herein.

96\. The NDA, executed by Oshiro on March 5, 2018, constitutes a valid, binding, and enforceable contract between CMT and Oshiro. The NDA was supported by adequate consideration, including CMT\'s offer of employment and Oshiro\'s access to CMT\'s Confidential Information. Oshiro\'s acceptance and performance under the NDA for approximately six years further confirms its validity and enforceability.

97\. Oshiro breached the NDA\'s non-disclosure provisions by disclosing CMT\'s Confidential Information---including the NeuralPath Algorithm, PrecisionDrive System, and PathPlanner Module---to Veridian, without CMT\'s authorization. Oshiro\'s disclosures included, but were not limited to, the transfer of approximately 4,700 proprietary files containing the CMT Trade Secrets to a personal USB drive and the subsequent provision of those files and/or the information contained therein to Veridian. The twenty-four (24)-month post-employment non-disclosure period runs through March 1, 2026. Oshiro\'s disclosures occurred and continue to occur well within this non-disclosure period.

98\. Oshiro breached the NDA\'s non-solicitation clause by directly or indirectly soliciting, inducing, recruiting, or encouraging CMT employees Dr. Kenji Furukawa, Samantha Briggs, and David Moreno to leave CMT and join Veridian. Dr. Furukawa resigned from CMT on April 22, 2024; Ms. Briggs resigned on May 8, 2024; and Mr. Moreno resigned on June 18, 2024. Each joined Veridian within weeks of their respective resignations. The eighteen (18)-month post-employment non-solicitation period runs through September 1, 2025. Each of these solicitations occurred well within the non-solicitation period.

99\. Oshiro breached the NDA\'s invention assignment clause to the extent that any inventions, improvements, discoveries, or works of authorship conceived, developed, or reduced to practice by Oshiro within twelve (12) months of the termination of his employment (i.e., through March 1, 2025) and relating to CMT\'s business are being claimed by, assigned to, or utilized by Veridian. Upon information and belief, Oshiro\'s work on the SynapticEdge platform includes inventions and developments that fall within the scope of the invention assignment clause.

100\. CMT has performed all conditions, covenants, and obligations required of it under the NDA. CMT has not breached any provision of the NDA and is not in default thereunder.

101\. As a direct and proximate result of Oshiro\'s breaches of the NDA, CMT has suffered damages in an amount to be proven at trial, but not less than \$82 million.

**COUNT IV: TORTIOUS INTERFERENCE WITH BUSINESS RELATIONS**

**(Against Defendant Veridian)**

102\. CMT incorporates by reference each and every allegation set forth in the preceding paragraphs as if fully restated herein.

103\. CMT had valid, existing, and prospective business relationships, including:

> \(a\) Licensing and sales relationships with twenty-eight (28) hospitals that were in active negotiations or had entered into letters of intent to license the NeuroGuide 4.0 platform; and
>
> \(b\) Employment relationships with Dr. Kenji Furukawa, Samantha Briggs, and David Moreno, each of whom was a valuable CMT employee with specialized knowledge and skills critical to CMT\'s NeuroGuide 4.0 development program.

104\. Veridian had knowledge of these business relationships, or, at a minimum, knowledge of facts and circumstances from which the existence of these relationships could reasonably be inferred. Veridian, as a direct competitor of CMT in the surgical robotics market, was aware of CMT\'s customer relationships and its employment of key engineering personnel.

105\. Veridian intentionally and improperly interfered with CMT\'s business relationships by:

> \(a\) Deliberately hiring Oshiro with the intent to acquire CMT\'s trade secrets and using those trade secrets to develop and announce the SynapticEdge platform, a competing product that directly disrupted CMT\'s hospital licensing relationships by undermining the exclusivity and competitive advantage of CMT\'s NeuroGuide 4.0 technology. As a direct result, twenty-eight hospitals delayed or cancelled their commitments to license the NeuroGuide 4.0 platform.
>
> \(b\) Knowingly facilitating, encouraging, and participating in Oshiro\'s solicitation of CMT employees Dr. Furukawa, Ms. Briggs, and Mr. Moreno, with knowledge of Oshiro\'s non-solicitation obligations under the NDA, or alternatively, independently recruiting these employees with knowledge that they were key members of CMT\'s NeuroGuide 4.0 development team and that their departure would cause significant harm to CMT.

106\. Veridian\'s interference was willful, intentional, and independently tortious---it was not merely incidental to the pursuit of Veridian\'s own legitimate business interests. Veridian\'s conduct involved the knowing exploitation of misappropriated trade secrets and the knowing facilitation of violations of Oshiro\'s contractual obligations to CMT.

107\. As a direct and proximate result of Veridian\'s tortious interference, CMT has suffered damages including at least \$34 million in lost licensing revenue, significant damage to its workforce and competitive position resulting from the loss of three key engineers, and other consequential damages to be proven at trial.

**[PRAYER FOR RELIEF]{.underline}**

WHEREFORE, Plaintiff Castillo Medical Technologies, Inc. respectfully requests that the Court enter judgment in its favor and against Defendants Ryan Oshiro and Veridian Health Systems, LLC, jointly and severally where applicable, and award CMT the following relief:

1\. Actual damages in an amount to be determined at trial, but not less than Eighty-Two Million Dollars (\$82,000,000);

2\. Exemplary damages under 18 U.S.C. § 1836(b)(3)(C) and/or Tex. Civ. Prac. & Rem. Code § 134A.004(b) for willful and malicious misappropriation, in an amount not exceeding two (2) times any award of actual damages;

3\. A permanent injunction:

> \(a\) Prohibiting Defendants, and all persons acting in concert with them, from using, disclosing, publishing, commercializing, or otherwise exploiting the NeuralPath Algorithm, PrecisionDrive System, PathPlanner Module, or any information, technology, or product derived therefrom;
>
> \(b\) Requiring Defendants to return to CMT or destroy, under CMT\'s supervision, all CMT Confidential Information in Defendants\' possession, custody, or control, including but not limited to all files contained on or derived from the Corsair USB drive bearing serial number CX-8827491 and any and all copies, reproductions, summaries, or derivative works thereof;
>
> \(c\) Prohibiting Defendant Oshiro from directly or indirectly soliciting, inducing, recruiting, or encouraging any employee of CMT to leave CMT\'s employment through the expiration of his non-solicitation obligation on September 1, 2025;

4\. Reasonable attorneys\' fees and costs incurred by CMT in this action under 18 U.S.C. § 1836(b)(3)(D) and/or applicable state law;

5\. Pre-judgment and post-judgment interest at the maximum rate allowed by law;

6\. Such other and further relief as the Court deems just and equitable.

**CONFIDENTIALITY, NON-DISCLOSURE, AND INVENTION ASSIGNMENT AGREEMENT**

This **CONFIDENTIALITY, NON-DISCLOSURE, AND INVENTION ASSIGNMENT AGREEMENT** (this \"Agreement\") is entered into and made effective as of the 5th day of March, 2018 (the \"Effective Date\"), by and between:

**CASTILLO MEDICAL TECHNOLOGIES, INC.**, a Delaware corporation, with its principal place of business at 4200 Lakeshore Parkway, Suite 800, Austin, TX 78746 (hereinafter referred to as the \"Company\" or \"CMT\"); and

**RYAN OSHIRO**, an individual residing in Travis County, Texas (hereinafter referred to as \"Employee\").

The Company and Employee may be referred to herein individually as a \"Party\" and collectively as the \"Parties.\"

**WHEREAS**, the Company is engaged in the design, manufacture, licensing, and commercialization of proprietary surgical robotic systems and related technology, and the Company has developed and continues to develop valuable confidential and proprietary information, trade secrets, and intellectual property in connection with its business;

**WHEREAS**, the Company desires to employ Employee in the position of Vice President of Engineering, commencing in March 2018, and Employee desires to accept such employment;

**WHEREAS**, in the course of Employee\'s duties as Vice President of Engineering, Employee will have access to and become acquainted with the Company\'s confidential and proprietary information, including trade secrets, the disclosure or misuse of which would cause serious and irreparable harm to the Company;

**WHEREAS**, as a condition of Employee\'s employment with the Company and in consideration of Employee\'s employment and Employee\'s access to the Company\'s Confidential Information (as defined below), the Parties desire to set forth their agreements regarding confidentiality, non-disclosure, non-solicitation, and the assignment of inventions;

**NOW, THEREFORE**, in consideration of the mutual covenants and agreements contained herein, the commencement and continuation of Employee\'s employment with the Company, Employee\'s access to Confidential Information, and other good and valuable consideration, the receipt and sufficiency of which are hereby acknowledged, the Parties agree as follows:

**[1. DEFINITIONS]{.underline}**

**1.1 \"Confidential Information\"** shall mean all information, whether written, oral, electronic, visual, or in any other form, that is proprietary to the Company or that the Company has received from third parties under an obligation of confidentiality, and that has or could have commercial value or other utility in the business in which the Company is engaged. Confidential Information includes, without limitation, all of the following categories of information and materials, whether or not marked or designated as \"confidential\" at the time of disclosure:

> \(a\) Technical data, algorithms, software (including source code and object code), designs, specifications, schematics, CAD files, engineering drawings, architecture documents, system diagrams, and technical documentation of any kind;
>
> \(b\) Inventions (whether or not patentable or reduced to practice), processes, techniques, methods, know-how, formulae, compositions, and discoveries;
>
> \(c\) Research and development information, experimental results, laboratory notebooks, prototypes, test data, validation results, clinical data, and pre-clinical data;
>
> \(d\) Product plans, product roadmaps, product architectures, product strategies, feature specifications, development timelines, and release schedules;
>
> \(e\) Machine learning models, training datasets, model weights, neural network architectures, AI/ML methodologies, inference pipelines, and related computational techniques;
>
> \(f\) Manufacturing processes, tooling designs, quality-control procedures, assembly instructions, calibration protocols, and supply chain information;
>
> \(g\) Customer lists, supplier lists, vendor lists, pricing information, cost structures, discount schedules, and marketing strategies and plans;
>
> \(h\) Financial information, budgets, forecasts, projections, revenue data, business plans, and strategic plans;
>
> \(i\) Personnel information, including the identities, skills, compensation, and roles of Company employees and contractors;
>
> \(j\) Any other information designated as confidential by the Company, whether disclosed orally, in writing, electronically, or by any other means;

including, without limitation, all information relating to the Company\'s surgical robotic platforms, neural mapping algorithms, robotic actuation systems, surgical planning software, and any related technologies, subsystems, components, and methodologies developed or under development by the Company. Confidential Information shall also include all notes, analyses, compilations, studies, summaries, memoranda, and other materials prepared by Employee that contain, reflect, or are derived from Confidential Information.

**1.2 Exclusions from Confidential Information.** Notwithstanding the foregoing, Confidential Information shall not include information that:

> \(a\) Is or becomes publicly available through no act, omission, or fault of Employee, provided that information shall not be deemed publicly available merely because it is embraced by more general information that is publicly available, or because individual components of such information are publicly available if the compilation or combination of such components is not publicly available;
>
> \(b\) Employee can demonstrate by clear and convincing written evidence was already known to Employee prior to Employee\'s employment with the Company, as evidenced by written records that predate the Effective Date of this Agreement;
>
> \(c\) Is independently developed by Employee without use of or reference to Confidential Information, as evidenced by contemporaneous written documentation created outside the scope of Employee\'s duties for the Company; or
>
> \(d\) Is received by Employee from a third party who is not, to Employee\'s knowledge, under any obligation of confidentiality to the Company with respect to such information.

**1.3 \"Company Materials\"** shall mean all documents, files, data, records, media, devices, equipment, and tangible or electronic materials containing or relating to Confidential Information, including but not limited to files stored on the Company\'s secure servers, repositories, internal systems, development environments, cloud platforms, laptops, mobile devices, and any other Company-owned or Company-managed systems, as well as all copies, reproductions, summaries, and extracts thereof.

**1.4 \"Inventions\"** shall mean all discoveries, inventions, improvements, designs, concepts, ideas, techniques, methods, systems, processes, software, algorithms, works of authorship, databases, mask works, trade secrets, and any other intellectual property, whether or not patentable, copyrightable, or otherwise protectable under applicable law.

**1.5 \"Prior Inventions\"** shall mean all Inventions that were made, conceived, reduced to practice, or created by Employee, either alone or jointly with others, prior to the Effective Date of this Agreement, which are identified and listed on Exhibit A attached hereto.

**1.6 \"Company\"** shall mean Castillo Medical Technologies, Inc. and its subsidiaries, affiliates, successors, and assigns.

**[2. NON-DISCLOSURE OBLIGATIONS]{.underline}**

**2.1 Duty of Confidentiality.** Employee agrees to hold all Confidential Information in the strictest confidence and to take all reasonable precautions to prevent the unauthorized disclosure, publication, dissemination, or distribution of Confidential Information. Employee shall not disclose, publish, disseminate, or otherwise make available any Confidential Information to any third party, whether during or after the period of Employee\'s employment with the Company, except as expressly authorized by the Company in writing or as reasonably necessary in the faithful performance of Employee\'s duties for the Company. Any disclosure made in the performance of Employee\'s duties shall be limited to those Company personnel or authorized third parties who have a legitimate need to know such information and who are bound by obligations of confidentiality no less restrictive than those contained herein.

**2.2 Use Restrictions.** Employee shall not use Confidential Information for any purpose other than the performance of Employee\'s duties for the Company. Without limiting the generality of the foregoing, Employee shall not use Confidential Information for the benefit of any other person or entity, including without limitation any subsequent employer, client, business partner, joint venture, or any business or enterprise in which Employee may have an interest, whether as an owner, partner, shareholder, officer, director, employee, consultant, advisor, or otherwise. Employee shall not copy, reproduce, reverse engineer, decompile, or disassemble any Confidential Information except as required in the course of Employee\'s authorized duties for the Company.

**2.3 Duration of Non-Disclosure Obligation.** The obligations under this Section 2 shall survive the termination of Employee\'s employment with the Company for any reason, whether voluntary or involuntary, with or without cause, and shall continue for a period of twenty-four (24) months following the last day of Employee\'s employment with the Company. For the avoidance of doubt, the non-disclosure period shall commence on the date of Employee\'s last day of employment and shall expire twenty-four (24) months thereafter. To the extent that any Confidential Information constitutes a \"trade secret\" under applicable law, including the Texas Uniform Trade Secrets Act or the Defend Trade Secrets Act of 2016 (18 U.S.C. § 1836 et seq.), Employee\'s obligations with respect to such trade secret shall continue for so long as such information remains a trade secret, regardless of whether the twenty-four (24)-month period has expired.

**2.4 Obligation to Protect.** Employee shall take all reasonable measures to protect the secrecy of and avoid disclosure or unauthorized use of Confidential Information, including at a minimum those measures Employee takes to protect Employee\'s own most sensitive confidential and personal information. Employee shall not remove Confidential Information from the Company\'s premises or systems except as strictly necessary for the performance of Employee\'s duties and in accordance with Company policies. Employee shall immediately notify the Company in writing of any unauthorized use or disclosure of Confidential Information of which Employee becomes aware, including any suspected breach by any person.

**2.5 Return of Company Materials.** Upon the termination of Employee\'s employment with the Company for any reason, or at any time upon the Company\'s written request, Employee shall promptly return to the Company all Company Materials in Employee\'s possession, custody, or control, including all originals, copies, summaries, extracts, and reproductions thereof, in whatever form, whether physical or electronic. Employee shall not retain any copies, reproductions, summaries, notes, or extracts of Company Materials in any medium. Employee shall also permanently and irrecoverably delete all Confidential Information from any personal devices, storage media (including external hard drives, USB drives, and flash drives), cloud accounts, personal email accounts, file-sharing platforms, or other systems under Employee\'s control. Upon request by the Company, Employee shall certify in writing that such return and deletion have been completed.

**2.6 Exit Certification.** Employee agrees to sign a certification upon termination of employment, in a form provided by the Company, confirming Employee\'s compliance with this Section 2, including without limitation the return of all Company Materials, the permanent deletion of all Confidential Information from personal systems, and Employee\'s continued understanding of and commitment to the obligations set forth in this Agreement. Employee\'s refusal or failure to sign such certification shall not relieve Employee of any obligation under this Agreement.

**[3. NON-SOLICITATION]{.underline}**

**3.1 Non-Solicitation of Employees.** For a period of eighteen (18) months following the last day of Employee\'s employment with the Company (the \"Employee Non-Solicitation Period\"), Employee shall not, directly or indirectly:

> \(a\) Solicit, recruit, hire, or attempt to solicit, recruit, or hire any person who is then employed by the Company, or who was employed by the Company at any time during the six (6) months preceding such solicitation, recruitment, or hiring;
>
> \(b\) Encourage, induce, or attempt to encourage or induce any employee of the Company to leave the Company\'s employment or to terminate or breach such employee\'s employment agreement or any other agreement with the Company; or
>
> \(c\) Assist any person, firm, corporation, or other entity in recruiting, soliciting, or hiring any employee of the Company, including by providing information about Company employees, their skills, compensation, or roles, or by facilitating introductions or interviews.

**3.2 Non-Solicitation of Customers and Business Partners.** For a period of eighteen (18) months following the last day of Employee\'s employment with the Company (the \"Customer Non-Solicitation Period\"), Employee shall not, directly or indirectly, solicit, divert, or attempt to solicit or divert the business of any customer, client, vendor, supplier, or business partner of the Company with whom Employee had material contact or about whom Employee obtained Confidential Information during the last twenty-four (24) months of Employee\'s employment with the Company, for the purpose of providing products or services competitive with those offered by the Company.

**3.3 Scope of \"Indirectly.\"** As used in this Section 3, the term \"indirectly\" includes, without limitation, any solicitation, recruitment, encouragement, inducement, or other prohibited activity undertaken through, on behalf of, at the direction of, or in coordination with a subsequent employer, agent, contractor, representative, or any other third party. Employee acknowledges that actions taken by a subsequent employer to solicit or recruit Company employees or customers may constitute indirect solicitation by Employee if Employee participates in, facilitates, assists, or directs such actions, or if Employee provides information or introductions that enable or contribute to such solicitation.

**3.4 Acknowledgments.** Employee acknowledges and agrees that:

> \(a\) In the course of Employee\'s employment, Employee will have access to information about Company employees, including their identities, skills, capabilities, compensation, and roles, and that such information constitutes Confidential Information protected under this Agreement;
>
> \(b\) The restrictions set forth in this Section 3 are reasonable and necessary in scope, duration, and geographic reach to protect the Company\'s legitimate business interests, including its investment in employee development, customer relationships, and Confidential Information;
>
> \(c\) Employee\'s compliance with the restrictions in this Section 3 will not impose undue hardship on Employee and will not prevent Employee from earning a livelihood; and
>
> \(d\) The Company would not have entered into this Agreement or commenced Employee\'s employment absent Employee\'s agreement to the covenants set forth in this Section 3.

**[4. INVENTION ASSIGNMENT]{.underline}**

**4.1 Assignment of Inventions During Employment.** Employee hereby irrevocably assigns and agrees to assign to the Company all right, title, and interest in and to all Inventions that Employee may solely or jointly conceive, develop, reduce to practice, or create during the period of Employee\'s employment with the Company, whether or not during working hours, whether or not at the Company\'s premises, and whether or not using Company equipment, supplies, facilities, or resources, if such Inventions: (a) relate to the Company\'s actual or reasonably anticipated business, research, or development at the time of conception or reduction to practice; or (b) result from any work performed by Employee for the Company, whether or not such work was assigned to Employee. This assignment shall include all intellectual property rights of any kind arising from such Inventions, including patent rights, copyrights, trade secret rights, mask work rights, and any other intellectual property rights recognized under the laws of any jurisdiction worldwide.

**4.2 Post-Employment Invention Assignment.** Employee hereby irrevocably assigns and agrees to assign to the Company all right, title, and interest in and to all Inventions that Employee may solely or jointly conceive, develop, reduce to practice, or create within twelve (12) months following the termination of Employee\'s employment with the Company, if such Inventions relate to the Company\'s business, research, or development and result from or are based upon Confidential Information to which Employee had access during Employee\'s employment. This twelve (12)-month post-employment assignment period shall commence on the date of Employee\'s last day of employment. Employee agrees that during such twelve (12)-month period, Employee shall promptly disclose to the Company any Inventions that may be subject to this Section 4.2 so that a determination of the Company\'s rights may be made.

**4.3 Disclosure of Inventions.** Employee shall promptly and fully disclose to the Company, in writing, all Inventions subject to assignment under this Section 4. Employee shall provide the Company with all information reasonably requested to evaluate and protect such Inventions and shall cooperate fully with the Company, at the Company\'s expense, in securing and prosecuting patents, copyrights, or other intellectual property protections for such Inventions in any and all jurisdictions. Such cooperation shall include, without limitation, executing patent applications, assignments, declarations, and any other documents the Company may reasonably request. These obligations shall survive the termination of Employee\'s employment.

**4.4 Prior Inventions Excluded.** Inventions identified and listed on Exhibit A attached hereto, if any, are excluded from the assignment obligations of this Section 4. If no Inventions are listed on Exhibit A, Employee represents and warrants that no such Prior Inventions exist and that all Inventions conceived, developed, reduced to practice, or created by Employee during employment shall be subject to assignment under this Section 4.

**4.5 Works Made for Hire.** To the extent that any Inventions constitute works of authorship, including software, documentation, and technical writings, Employee agrees that such works are \"works made for hire\" as defined under the Copyright Act of 1976 (17 U.S.C. § 101) and shall be the sole and exclusive property of the Company. To the extent that any such work is determined not to be a work made for hire, Employee hereby irrevocably assigns to the Company all right, title, and interest in and to such work, including all copyrights therein.

**4.6 Moral Rights Waiver.** To the maximum extent permitted by applicable law, Employee hereby irrevocably waives and agrees never to assert any and all moral rights Employee may have in any Inventions assigned to the Company under this Agreement, including any rights of attribution, integrity, disclosure, and withdrawal.

**[5. REPRESENTATIONS AND ACKNOWLEDGMENTS]{.underline}**

**5.1 No Conflicting Obligations.** Employee represents and warrants that Employee is not a party to any agreement, understanding, obligation, or duty, whether written or oral, that would conflict with or prevent Employee from fully performing Employee\'s obligations under this Agreement. Employee further represents that Employee\'s execution of this Agreement and performance of Employee\'s duties for the Company will not breach any agreement with any prior employer, client, or third party or any duty of confidentiality owed to any prior employer, client, or third party.

**5.2 No Prior Employer Materials.** Employee represents and warrants that Employee has not brought and will not bring to the Company, or use in the performance of Employee\'s duties for the Company, any confidential, proprietary, or trade secret information belonging to any prior employer or third party, unless Employee has obtained proper written authorization from such prior employer or third party to use or disclose such information. Employee understands that the Company does not want and has not requested that Employee provide any such information.

**5.3 Understanding of Obligations.** Employee acknowledges that Employee has carefully read and fully understands this Agreement, that Employee has had the opportunity to consult with independent legal counsel of Employee\'s own choosing prior to executing this Agreement, and that Employee voluntarily and knowingly enters into this Agreement. Employee acknowledges that the restrictions and obligations contained in this Agreement are reasonable and necessary to protect the Company\'s legitimate business interests.

**5.4 Ongoing Obligations to Prior Employers.** Employee acknowledges that Employee may have continuing obligations to prior employers, including obligations of confidentiality and non-disclosure, and Employee agrees to honor those obligations. The Company will not direct Employee to breach any such obligations and Employee agrees not to do so. Employee shall inform the Company if Employee believes that any task or assignment may require Employee to breach any obligation to a prior employer.

**[6. REMEDIES]{.underline}**

**6.1 Irreparable Harm.** Employee acknowledges and agrees that any breach or threatened breach of this Agreement may cause the Company irreparable harm for which monetary damages alone would be an inadequate remedy. Accordingly, the Company shall be entitled to seek and obtain injunctive relief (including temporary restraining orders, preliminary injunctions, and permanent injunctions), specific performance, and any other equitable remedies from any court of competent jurisdiction, in addition to any and all other rights and remedies available to the Company at law or in equity. Employee agrees that the Company shall not be required to post a bond or other security as a condition of obtaining such equitable relief.

**6.2 No Waiver of Rights.** The Company\'s failure or delay in enforcing any provision of this Agreement shall not constitute a waiver of the Company\'s right to enforce such provision or any other provision of this Agreement at any time thereafter. No waiver of any breach of this Agreement shall be deemed a waiver of any subsequent or continuing breach.

**6.3 Attorneys\' Fees.** In the event of any action, suit, or proceeding to enforce or interpret this Agreement, the prevailing party shall be entitled to recover its reasonable attorneys\' fees, costs, and expenses, including fees and costs incurred in connection with any appeals.

**6.4 Tolling.** The restricted periods set forth in Sections 2 and 3 of this Agreement shall be tolled during any period in which Employee is in breach of such provisions. The tolling shall continue until such breach is cured or the breach is adjudicated by a court of competent jurisdiction, and the applicable restricted period shall resume and continue for the remaining duration upon the cessation of such breach.

**[7. GENERAL PROVISIONS]{.underline}**

**7.1 Governing Law.** This Agreement shall be governed by, construed, and enforced in accordance with the laws of the State of Texas, without regard to its principles of conflicts of law that would result in the application of the laws of any other jurisdiction.

**7.2 Jurisdiction and Venue.** The Parties hereby irrevocably consent to the exclusive jurisdiction and venue of the state and federal courts located in Travis County, Texas, for any action, suit, or proceeding arising out of or relating to this Agreement. Each Party waives any objection to the laying of venue in such courts and any claim that any such action, suit, or proceeding has been brought in an inconvenient forum.

**7.3 Entire Agreement.** This Agreement, including Exhibit A attached hereto and incorporated herein by reference, constitutes the entire agreement between the Parties with respect to the subject matter hereof and supersedes all prior and contemporaneous agreements, understandings, negotiations, discussions, and representations, whether written or oral, relating to such subject matter. No terms or conditions contained in any offer letter, employment agreement, or other document shall modify or supersede the terms of this Agreement unless such document expressly references this Agreement and is signed by both Parties.

**7.4 Amendments.** This Agreement may not be amended, modified, or supplemented except by a written instrument executed by both Parties. No oral modification of this Agreement shall be effective.

**7.5 Severability.** If any provision of this Agreement, or the application thereof to any person or circumstance, is held to be invalid, illegal, or unenforceable by a court of competent jurisdiction, such holding shall not affect the validity, legality, or enforceability of the remaining provisions of this Agreement, which shall continue in full force and effect. In the event that any provision is held to be overbroad or unreasonable in scope, duration, or geographic reach, the invalid provision shall be reformed and construed to the minimum extent necessary to render it valid, legal, and enforceable while preserving the original intent of the Parties to the greatest extent possible.

**7.6 Assignment.** The Company may freely assign this Agreement, in whole or in part, to any successor, affiliate, or assignee of all or substantially all of the Company\'s business or assets, without the consent of Employee, and this Agreement shall inure to the benefit of such successor, affiliate, or assignee. Employee may not assign this Agreement or any rights or obligations hereunder without the prior written consent of the Company.

**7.7 Survival.** The obligations and rights set forth in Sections 1 through 6 of this Agreement shall survive the termination of Employee\'s employment with the Company for any reason, whether voluntary or involuntary, with or without cause, and shall remain in full force and effect in accordance with their terms.

**7.8 Notices.** All notices, demands, and other communications required or permitted under this Agreement shall be in writing and shall be deemed duly given when: (a) delivered personally; (b) sent by confirmed electronic mail; (c) sent by nationally recognized overnight courier service; or (d) sent by certified or registered mail, postage prepaid, return receipt requested, to the addresses of the Parties set forth in the preamble to this Agreement or to such other address as either Party may hereafter designate in writing. Notices to the Company shall be directed to the attention of the General Counsel.

**7.9 Counterparts.** This Agreement may be executed in two or more counterparts, each of which shall be deemed an original, and all of which together shall constitute one and the same instrument. Execution of this Agreement by facsimile or electronic signature shall be deemed valid and binding for all purposes.
