**SCHEDULING ORDER**

Before the Honorable Rebecca L. Hightower United States District Judge

Date of Order: September 4, 2024

**[I. PREAMBLE]{.underline}**

On August 30, 2024, the Court held a scheduling conference pursuant to Rule 16(b) of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure, having received and reviewed the parties\' Joint Report of the Parties\' Planning Meeting filed pursuant to Rule 26(f) on August 23, 2024. Counsel for all parties appeared at the conference. Plaintiff Castillo Medical Technologies, Inc. (\"CMT\" or \"Plaintiff\") was represented by Margaret \"Meg\" Hargrove and Kevin Nakamura of Hargrove, Pell & Sutton LLP, 2900 Elm Street, 40th Floor, Dallas, Texas 75201. Defendants Veridian Health Systems, LLC (\"Veridian\") and Ryan Oshiro (\"Oshiro\") (collectively, \"Defendants\") were represented by Stephen Osei of Drummond & Wakefield LLP, 1150 K Street NW, Suite 1200, Washington, DC 20005. Counsel for all Defendants appeared and participated in the conference.

The Court has considered the nature and complexity of the claims asserted in this action, which include trade secret misappropriation under the Defend Trade Secrets Act, 18 U.S.C. § 1836 *et seq.* (\"DTSA\"), and the Texas Uniform Trade Secrets Act, Tex. Civ. Prac. & Rem. Code § 134A.001 *et seq.* (\"TUTSA\"), as well as claims for breach of contract (non-disclosure agreement) and tortious interference with business relations. The Court further notes that a Temporary Restraining Order was entered in this matter on August 2, 2024, and that a preliminary injunction hearing is currently scheduled for October 15, 2024. The Court has considered the complexity of the subject matter --- which involves proprietary surgical robotics technology, including specifically identified trade secrets --- the anticipated volume of electronically stored information likely at issue, the parties\' relative resources, and the need for efficient case management. Having considered all of the foregoing, the submissions of the parties, and good cause appearing, the Court hereby enters the following Scheduling Order pursuant to Fed. R. Civ. P. 16(b).

**[II. DISCOVERY PLAN AND DEADLINES]{.underline}**

**A. General Discovery Period**

The discovery period shall commence on the date of this Order, September 4, 2024, and shall close on **April 30, 2025**. All discovery must be initiated in sufficient time to be completed --- that is, all responses must be received and all depositions must be taken --- by the close of the discovery period, unless this Order specifically provides otherwise. Extensions of the discovery period will be granted only upon a showing of good cause pursuant to Fed. R. Civ. P. 16(b)(4), and the parties should not assume that such extensions will be forthcoming.

**B. Written Discovery --- Interrogatories**

1\. **Interrogatory Limit and Subpart Counting.** The Court explicitly reaffirms that each party is limited to **twenty-five (25) interrogatories, including all discrete subparts**, directed to any other party, pursuant to Fed. R. Civ. P. 33(a)(1). In accordance with this Court\'s practice, the Court adopts the position that subparts that are not logically or factually related to the primary interrogatory will be counted as separate interrogatories. A subpart is logically or factually related to the primary interrogatory only if it seeks information on the same subject matter and could reasonably be considered a natural component of a single, unified inquiry. Parties are cautioned that the Court will strictly enforce this limit, and any interrogatory set that exceeds twenty-five (25) interrogatories --- as counted under this standard --- may be stricken in whole or in part upon motion by the opposing party, without further notice or opportunity to cure. Leave of Court is required to serve additional interrogatories beyond the twenty-five-interrogatory limit, and such leave will be granted only upon a showing of good cause and a particularized need for the additional discovery that cannot be obtained through other, less burdensome means.

2\. **Service Deadline.** All interrogatories shall be served no later than **January 2, 2025** (one hundred twenty days from the date of this Order). Responses shall be due thirty (30) days after service, consistent with Fed. R. Civ. P. 33(b)(2).

3\. **Proportionality and Relevance.** The Court reminds all parties that all discovery, including interrogatories, must be proportional to the needs of the case, considering the importance of the issues at stake in the action, the amount in controversy, the parties\' relative access to relevant information, the parties\' resources, the importance of the discovery in resolving the issues, and whether the burden or expense of the proposed discovery outweighs its likely benefit. Fed. R. Civ. P. 26(b)(1). The Court notes that the claims in this action involve specifically identified trade secrets --- namely, the NeuralPath Algorithm, the PrecisionDrive System, and the PathPlanner Module --- and discovery should be focused on matters relevant to these identified trade secrets and to the claims and defenses set forth in the First Amended Complaint. Discovery requests directed at the parties\' general business operations, unrelated product lines, or technologies not at issue in this litigation will not be countenanced. Overly broad or unduly burdensome discovery requests that are not tailored to the specific claims and defenses at issue in this case will be subject to appropriate sanctions and protective measures.

**C. Written Discovery --- Requests for Production and Requests for Admission**

Requests for production of documents pursuant to Fed. R. Civ. P. 34 shall be served no later than **January 2, 2025**. Responses and production shall be due thirty (30) days after service, in accordance with the Federal Rules. Requests for admission pursuant to Fed. R. Civ. P. 36 shall be served no later than **March 1, 2025**. Responses to requests for admission shall be due thirty (30) days after service. The Court\'s proportionality instruction set forth in Section II.B.3 of this Order applies with equal force to all written discovery, including requests for production and requests for admission. The parties are further reminded of their obligations under Fed. R. Civ. P. 26(g) to certify that all discovery requests and responses are consistent with the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure, are not interposed for any improper purpose --- including to harass, cause unnecessary delay, or needlessly increase the cost of litigation --- and are neither unreasonable nor unduly burdensome or expensive, considering the needs of the case, prior discovery in the case, the amount in controversy, and the importance of the issues at stake.

**D. Depositions.** Depositions shall not commence until after the completion of written discovery responses --- that is, no depositions shall be noticed or taken before the written discovery response period has elapsed for the initial sets of written discovery served in this action, unless otherwise agreed by the parties in writing or ordered by the Court. Each side shall be limited to **ten (10) depositions** of seven (7) hours each, consistent with Fed. R. Civ. P. 30(a)(2)(A)(i) and 30(d)(1). For purposes of this limitation, Defendants Oshiro and Veridian shall be treated collectively as one \"side.\" All fact depositions must be completed by **April 30, 2025**, the close of the general discovery period. The parties shall confer in good faith regarding a mutually agreeable deposition schedule and shall submit a joint proposed deposition schedule to the Court no later than **February 15, 2025**. In the event the parties are unable to agree on a deposition schedule, each side shall submit a proposed schedule by that date, and the Court will resolve any conflicts.

**E. Expert Discovery**

Plaintiff\'s initial expert reports, complying in all respects with the requirements of Fed. R. Civ. P. 26(a)(2)(B), shall be served no later than **March 15, 2025**. Defendants\' responsive expert reports, also complying with Rule 26(a)(2)(B), shall be served no later than **April 15, 2025**. Plaintiff\'s rebuttal expert reports, if any, limited in scope to matters raised in Defendants\' responsive expert reports, shall be served no later than **April 30, 2025**. All expert depositions shall be completed by **May 31, 2025**. The Court extends this deadline beyond the general discovery close date for expert depositions only; no other discovery shall be conducted after April 30, 2025, absent further order of the Court.

**[III. PRELIMINARY INJUNCTION HEARING]{.underline}**

The preliminary injunction hearing remains scheduled for **October 15, 2024**, as set by the Court\'s August 2, 2024 Order granting Plaintiff\'s application for a Temporary Restraining Order. The parties may conduct expedited, limited discovery related to the preliminary injunction motion prior to the hearing date, subject to the proportionality requirements of this Order and Fed. R. Civ. P. 26(b)(1). Such expedited discovery shall be narrowly tailored to the issues presented by the pending motion for preliminary injunction. Any disputes regarding expedited discovery for the preliminary injunction hearing shall be brought to the Court\'s attention via letter brief not to exceed three (3) pages, with at least twenty-four (24) hours\' notice to opposing counsel prior to submission.

**[IV. DISPOSITIVE MOTIONS]{.underline}**

All dispositive motions, including motions for summary judgment pursuant to Fed. R. Civ. P. 56, shall be filed no later than **June 30, 2025**. Responses to dispositive motions shall be due twenty-one (21) days after the filing of the motion. Reply briefs, if any, shall be due fourteen (14) days after the response is filed. All briefing shall comply with the page limitations set forth in the Local Rules of this Court. The Court will endeavor to rule on all dispositive motions no later than **August 15, 2025**, in advance of the scheduled trial date set forth in Section V below.

**[V. PRETRIAL CONFERENCE AND TRIAL]{.underline}**

A **final pretrial conference** shall be held on **August 25, 2025**, at 2:00 p.m. in Courtroom 3 of the United States Courthouse, Austin, Texas. Lead trial counsel for all parties must attend the final pretrial conference in person. The parties shall submit a **joint pretrial order**, prepared in accordance with this Court\'s Standing Order on Pretrial Submissions, no later than **August 18, 2025**. The joint pretrial order shall include, among other things, stipulations of fact, contested issues of fact and law, exhibit lists, witness lists, and any objections thereto.

All **motions in limine** shall be filed no later than **August 18, 2025**. Responses to motions in limine shall be due seven (7) days after filing, unless the Court orders otherwise at the final pretrial conference.

**Trial** is set to commence on **September 8, 2025**. The trial is estimated to last approximately ten (10) court days (two calendar weeks). The format of the trial --- bench or jury --- shall be addressed at the final pretrial conference based upon the demands of the parties as reflected in the pleadings and any jury demand filed in accordance with Fed. R. Civ. P. 38. Counsel should be prepared to discuss the anticipated length of their respective cases-in-chief and any logistical issues related to the presentation of evidence at the final pretrial conference.

**[VI. PRESERVATION OF EVIDENCE AND ELECTRONICALLY STORED INFORMATION]{.underline}**

The Court reminds all parties and their counsel of their independent obligations to preserve all relevant evidence, including electronically stored information (\"ESI\"), from the date each party knew or reasonably should have known that litigation was reasonably anticipated. These preservation obligations are continuing and extend to all documents, communications, and data that may be relevant to the claims and defenses asserted in this action.

The parties shall meet and confer regarding an **ESI protocol** governing the identification, preservation, collection, review, and production of ESI no later than **October 1, 2024**. The parties shall submit an agreed ESI protocol to the Court no later than **October 8, 2024**. If the parties are unable to agree on all provisions of an ESI protocol, each side shall submit a proposed protocol by that date, together with a brief statement (not to exceed three pages) identifying the areas of disagreement and the basis for each side\'s position. The ESI protocol shall address, at a minimum: the identification of custodians whose ESI will be searched and collected; the search terms or other methodologies to be employed; applicable date ranges; the sources of ESI to be searched, including email accounts, messaging platforms (such as Slack, Microsoft Teams, or similar applications), cloud storage services, shared network drives, and removable media; and the format of production (including whether production will be made in native format, TIFF images, or other agreed-upon format).

Failure to preserve relevant ESI may result in sanctions under Fed. R. Civ. P. 37(e), including, where appropriate, adverse inference instructions, preclusion of evidence, or other remedies the Court deems just. The Court notes that a litigation hold letter was sent by Plaintiff\'s counsel to Defendants on June 28, 2024, and that the Temporary Restraining Order entered by this Court on August 2, 2024, separately imposed specific preservation obligations on Defendants. Defendants are expected to comply fully with all such obligations.

**[VII. CONFIDENTIALITY / PROTECTIVE ORDER]{.underline}**

Given the nature of the claims asserted in this action, which involve trade secrets and proprietary surgical robotics technology, the parties shall submit an **agreed proposed protective order** to the Court for approval no later than **September 25, 2024**. If the parties are unable to agree on all terms of a protective order by that date, each side shall submit a proposed protective order, together with a brief explanation of any unresolved disputes, by September 25, 2024, and the Court will resolve the disputes by separate order. The protective order shall include, at a minimum, provisions for designating materials as \"Confidential\" and \"Attorneys\' Eyes Only,\" procedures for challenging confidentiality designations, and provisions governing the handling, use, and return or destruction of trade secret and other proprietary information produced during discovery and at trial.

**[VIII. DISCOVERY DISPUTES]{.underline}**

Before filing any discovery motion, the parties must **meet and confer in good faith** to attempt to resolve the dispute without Court intervention, as required by Local Rule CV-7(h) of the Western District of Texas and Fed. R. Civ. P. 37(a)(1). The meet-and-confer effort shall include, at a minimum, a live conversation (in person or by telephone) between counsel with authority to resolve the dispute; an exchange of emails alone is insufficient.

If the dispute cannot be resolved through the meet-and-confer process, the aggrieved party may file a **letter brief** not to exceed **five (5) pages** setting forth the nature of the dispute, the parties\' respective positions, a summary of the meet-and-confer efforts undertaken, and the specific relief sought. The opposing party shall have three (3) business days to respond by letter brief of equal length. The Court will address discovery disputes promptly, by telephone conference or written order, without the need for full briefing in most instances.

The Court will evaluate all discovery disputes through the lens of Fed. R. Civ. P. 26(b)(1) proportionality. The party seeking to compel discovery bears the initial burden of demonstrating that the discovery sought is relevant to any party\'s claim or defense and is proportional to the needs of the case. The party resisting discovery bears the burden of demonstrating that the discovery sought is unduly burdensome, disproportionate, or otherwise not permitted under the Federal Rules. The Court expects the parties to approach discovery cooperatively and in a spirit of candor, and will not hesitate to impose sanctions, including the award of fees and costs, upon any party or counsel whose conduct falls short of these expectations.

**[IX. AMENDMENTS TO PLEADINGS AND JOINDER]{.underline}**

Any motions to amend pleadings or to join additional parties shall be filed no later than **November 15, 2024**. After this date, motions to amend or to join additional parties will be permitted only upon a showing of good cause under Fed. R. Civ. P. 16(b)(4) and, where applicable, the standards of Fed. R. Civ. P. 15(a)(2). The parties are reminded that the good cause standard under Rule 16(b)(4) focuses primarily on the diligence of the party seeking modification, and that a lack of diligence will weigh heavily against any request to amend pleadings or join parties after the deadline set by this Order.

**[X. SETTLEMENT AND ALTERNATIVE DISPUTE RESOLUTION]{.underline}**

The parties are encouraged to explore settlement at all stages of the litigation. The Court reminds the parties that early and meaningful settlement discussions can conserve the resources of the parties and the Court. The parties shall participate in at least one **mediation session** no later than **March 31, 2025**, unless excused by the Court upon a showing of good cause. The parties shall jointly select a mediator and notify the Court of the mediator\'s identity and the scheduled mediation date no later than **January 15, 2025**. If the parties are unable to agree upon a mediator by that date, the Court will appoint a mediator from its roster of qualified neutrals. All parties and representatives with full settlement authority must attend the mediation in person.

**[XI. MODIFICATION OF THIS ORDER]{.underline}**

This Scheduling Order may be modified only upon a showing of **good cause** pursuant to Fed. R. Civ. P. 16(b)(4). Stipulations of the parties alone are insufficient to modify this Order; Court approval is required for any modification. Counsel are expected to plan their case activities to comply with the deadlines set forth in this Order and should not assume that extensions will be granted. Requests for extension filed on the eve of a deadline, without a showing of diligence, will be viewed with disfavor.

**IT IS SO ORDERED.**

SIGNED this 4th day of September, 2024.

**[DIGITAL FORENSICS INVESTIGATION REPORT]{.underline}**

**Prepared for Castillo Medical Technologies, Inc.**

 

Report Number: ADF-2024-0419

Date of Report: May 15, 2024

 

Prepared by:

**ASHCROFT DIGITAL FORENSICS, LLC** 7600 Ridgeline Boulevard, Suite 210 Round Rock, TX 78681

 

Lead Forensic Examiner:

**Nathan Cordero, EnCE, CFCE** Lead Forensic Examiner

 

 

CONFIDENTIAL --- ATTORNEY WORK PRODUCT / PREPARED IN ANTICIPATION OF LITIGATION This report was prepared at the direction of legal counsel in anticipation of litigation and is protected by the attorney work product doctrine.

Right-click to update Table of Contents

**[I. EXECUTIVE SUMMARY]{.underline}**

Ashcroft Digital Forensics, LLC (\"Ashcroft\" or \"ADF\") was retained by Castillo Medical Technologies, Inc. (\"CMT\"), through its litigation counsel Hargrove, Pell & Sutton LLP, on March 12, 2024, to conduct a comprehensive forensic investigation of the digital activities of Ryan Oshiro, CMT\'s former Vice President of Engineering, during his final weeks of employment at the company. The purpose of the engagement was to determine whether Oshiro engaged in unauthorized copying, downloading, or transfer of CMT proprietary files from CMT\'s systems during the period preceding his departure.

Oshiro served as CMT\'s Vice President of Engineering from approximately March 2018 through March 1, 2024, the date of his final day of employment. In that capacity, Oshiro held administrator-level access to CMT\'s secure engineering repository, known internally as \"VaultDrive,\" which houses the company\'s most sensitive proprietary technical data related to its flagship NeuroGuide surgical navigation platform.

The investigation focused on two primary sources of digital evidence: (1) Oshiro\'s CMT-issued laptop, a Dell Precision 5570 mobile workstation bearing CMT asset tag CMT-ENG-4417 and serial number DELLSN-JK7829401; and (2) access logs from the VaultDrive system for Oshiro\'s account for the period October 1, 2023, through March 1, 2024.

**Key Finding --- Data Exfiltration to Personal USB Drive.** Between January 8, 2024, and February 28, 2024, Oshiro downloaded approximately 4,700 files totaling approximately 38.4 gigabytes (\"GB\") of data from VaultDrive to a personal external USB storage device --- specifically, a 2TB Corsair portable drive bearing serial number CX-8827491. This personal USB drive had never been connected to the CMT laptop prior to January 8, 2024, and is not a CMT-issued device. The downloaded files encompassed four categories of CMT proprietary material:

> \(1\) CAD schematics and mechanical design files for the PrecisionDrive System (approximately 1,200 files);
>
> \(2\) Source code for the NeuralPath Algorithm (approximately 2,100 files);
>
> \(3\) PathPlanner Module training datasets, model weights, and configuration files (approximately 800 files); and
>
> \(4\) Internal R&D roadmap documents and competitive intelligence memoranda (approximately 600 files).

These four categories collectively represent the core proprietary technical portfolio underlying CMT\'s NeuroGuide 4.0 platform, including all three trade secrets identified by CMT in this matter: the NeuralPath Algorithm, the PrecisionDrive System, and the PathPlanner Module.

**Key Finding --- Attempted Log Deletion.** On February 27, 2024, at approximately 9:14 PM CST, Oshiro\'s VaultDrive account executed a series of administrative commands designed to delete the access logs associated with his account, in an apparent effort to conceal the scope of his download activity. The deletion command was partially successful in clearing the primary VaultDrive audit log database. However, the deletion was captured and preserved in its entirety by CMT\'s automated backup system, the \"VaultDrive Shadow Archive,\" which mirrors all VaultDrive logs to a separate, read-only storage server that is not accessible to individual user accounts. The Shadow Archive retained a complete record of all 4,700-plus file download events, as well as the deletion attempt itself.

**Key Finding --- Pattern Consistent with Systematic Exfiltration.** The pattern, volume, and timing of Oshiro\'s file downloads are consistent with systematic, deliberate exfiltration of proprietary technical data and are fundamentally inconsistent with routine work activity. The bulk download activity commenced on January 8, 2024, approximately five weeks before Oshiro submitted his resignation on February 14, 2024, and continued through February 28, 2024 --- two days before his final day of employment. The downloads were concentrated in 14 discrete sessions corresponding precisely to the 14 dates the personal USB drive was connected to the CMT laptop. This pattern reflects a methodical campaign to acquire the totality of CMT\'s NeuroGuide 4.0 proprietary technical data before Oshiro\'s departure from the company.

The findings of this investigation are set forth in detail in the sections that follow.

**[IV. METHODOLOGY]{.underline}**

**IV.A. Evidence Acquisition**

On March 14, 2024, Nathan Cordero of Ashcroft traveled to CMT\'s headquarters at 4200 Lakeshore Parkway, Suite 800, Austin, TX 78746, to receive and take custody of Oshiro\'s CMT-issued laptop. The laptop --- a Dell Precision 5570 mobile workstation, CMT asset tag CMT-ENG-4417, serial number DELLSN-JK7829401 --- was delivered to Mr. Cordero by Greg Halverson, CMT\'s Director of Information Technology, who had maintained custody of the device since it was collected from Oshiro on March 1, 2024. A chain-of-custody form was executed by both Mr. Cordero and Mr. Halverson at the time of transfer. The laptop was placed in a sealed anti-static evidence bag and transported to Ashcroft\'s forensic laboratory in Round Rock, Texas.

Upon arrival at Ashcroft\'s laboratory, the laptop was logged into Ashcroft\'s evidence management system and stored in a locked evidence storage room accessible only to authorized forensic personnel. On March 15, 2024, a forensically sound bit-for-bit image of the laptop\'s internal 1TB NVMe solid-state drive was created using EnCase Forensic version 8.12. The imaging process was performed using a Tableau T35689iu forensic write-blocker to ensure that no data was written to or altered on the original drive during the imaging process. MD5 and SHA-256 hash values were calculated for both the original drive and the resulting forensic image immediately upon completion of imaging. The hash values for the original and the image matched exactly, confirming the integrity and completeness of the forensic copy. All subsequent analysis was performed on the forensic image; the original laptop and its drive were returned to sealed evidence storage and have not been altered.

Separately, VaultDrive server access logs were exported by CMT\'s IT department on March 15, 2024, under the supervision of Greg Halverson. The logs covered the period from October 1, 2023, through March 1, 2024, and were delivered to Ashcroft on a 256-bit AES-encrypted external drive on the same date. A chain-of-custody form was executed for this evidence as well. A second set of logs --- specifically, the VaultDrive Shadow Archive backup logs covering the same period --- was exported by CMT IT on March 18, 2024, and delivered to Ashcroft on a separate encrypted external drive on that date. Chain-of-custody documentation was maintained for each evidence transfer. Hash values were computed for all log files upon receipt and again upon the commencement of analysis, confirming that no changes occurred during storage.

**IV.B. Analysis Tools**

The following tools and software were used in the course of this forensic investigation:

> • **EnCase Forensic v8.12** --- used for disk imaging, file system analysis, artifact extraction, and keyword searching on the forensic image of Oshiro\'s laptop;
>
> • **Forensic Toolkit (FTK) v7.6** --- used for file carving, metadata extraction, and supplemental analysis of recovered files and artifacts;
>
> • **USB Forensic Tracker v2.1** --- used for extraction and analysis of USB device connection history from Windows Registry hives, Setup API logs, and Event Logs;
>
> • **Registry Explorer v2.0** --- used for detailed analysis of Windows Registry hives, including SYSTEM, SOFTWARE, NTUSER.DAT, and UsrClass.dat, to identify USB device artifacts, user activity artifacts, and system configuration data;
>
> • **Custom Python scripts** --- developed by Ashcroft for the purpose of correlating VaultDrive access log entries with laptop file system artifacts and USB connection timestamps, and for generating timeline visualizations of data transfer activity; and
>
> • **Microsoft Excel and Tableau Desktop** --- used for statistical analysis of access patterns and for generating comparative charts of baseline versus anomalous activity.

**IV.C. Analysis Steps**

The forensic analysis was conducted in the following sequence:

> 1\. **Windows Registry Analysis.** Registry hives from the forensic image were analyzed to identify all USB mass storage devices ever connected to the laptop. Key registry locations analyzed included HKLM\\SYSTEM\\CurrentControlSet\\Enum\\USBSTOR, HKLM\\SYSTEM\\CurrentControlSet\\Enum\\USB, and the user-specific NTUSER.DAT hive for MountPoints2 entries. SetupAPI logs and Windows Event Logs were also analyzed for corroboration.
>
> 2\. **Timeline Reconstruction.** File system timestamps, Shellbags, Jump Lists, and Windows LNK (shortcut) files were analyzed to reconstruct a timeline of file copy operations from the laptop to external USB devices. NTFS \$MFT (Master File Table) entries and \$UsnJrnl (USN Change Journal) records were parsed to identify file creation, modification, and access events on the external drive.
>
> 3\. **VaultDrive Access Log Analysis.** The VaultDrive server access logs were parsed and filtered for all events associated with Oshiro\'s account (roshiro@castillomedtech.com). Each event was categorized by type (file view, file download, directory listing, administrative command) and timestamped.
>
> 4\. **Cross-Referencing.** VaultDrive file download events were cross-referenced with USB device connection timestamps from the laptop to identify temporal correlations between VaultDrive downloads and USB drive presence.
>
> 5\. **Shadow Archive Analysis.** The VaultDrive Shadow Archive logs were compared against the primary VaultDrive logs to identify any discrepancies resulting from log deletion or tampering. The Shadow Archive was independently verified for integrity using daily hash snapshots maintained by CMT\'s backup system.
>
> 6\. **Metadata Extraction.** Metadata from files identified in the VaultDrive download logs was extracted to determine file types, sizes, creation dates, modification dates, and directory paths. This metadata was used to categorize the downloaded files by subject matter and functional area.

All work was performed in accordance with the guidelines set forth in NIST Special Publication 800-86, *Guide to Integrating Forensic Techniques into Incident Response*, and in compliance with Ashcroft Digital Forensics\' internal Standard Operating Procedures for evidence handling, forensic imaging, and forensic analysis.

**[V. FINDINGS]{.underline}**

**V.A. USB Device Identification**

Analysis of the Windows Registry hives extracted from the forensic image of Oshiro\'s CMT-issued laptop revealed that a USB mass storage device was first connected to the laptop on January 8, 2024, at 07:42:18 CST. This connection event was recorded in the HKLM\\SYSTEM\\CurrentControlSet\\Enum\\USBSTOR registry key and corroborated by corresponding entries in the Windows SetupAPI device installation log (C:\\Windows\\INF\\setupapi.dev.log) and Windows Event Log entries (Microsoft-Windows-DriverFrameworks-UserMode/Operational).

The device was identified through its USB Device Descriptor as a Corsair portable external storage drive with a capacity of 2 terabytes (2TB). The device\'s unique serial number, as recorded in the USB Device Descriptor and the USBSTOR registry key, is **CX-8827491**. The device was formatted with the NTFS file system and was consistently assigned drive letter \"E:\\\" on the laptop upon each connection. The Registry\'s MountPoints2 entry in Oshiro\'s NTUSER.DAT hive confirmed the assignment of the E:\\ mount point to this specific device.

A thorough examination of the Windows Registry, SetupAPI logs, and Event Logs confirmed that no personal external USB mass storage devices were connected to Oshiro\'s laptop at any point during the period under review (October 1, 2023, through March 1, 2024) other than USB drive CX-8827491. CMT-issued encrypted backup drives were connected to the laptop periodically during this period in accordance with CMT\'s standard backup protocols; these devices are identified by CMT-assigned serial numbers and are excluded from this analysis.

The USB drive CX-8827491 was connected to the laptop on a total of **fourteen (14) separate dates** between January 8, 2024, and February 28, 2024. The specific dates, connection and disconnection times, and approximate data transfer volumes are set forth in the table in Section V.B below. After February 28, 2024, the USB drive was not connected to the laptop again. The laptop was collected by CMT\'s IT department on March 1, 2024, at approximately 5:15 PM CST, upon Oshiro\'s departure from CMT\'s offices on his final day of employment.

The USB drive CX-8827491 was not returned to CMT and was not among the items recovered from Oshiro\'s office or workstation. Its current location and custodian are unknown to Ashcroft.

**V.C. File Download Analysis --- VaultDrive Access Logs**

VaultDrive is CMT\'s secure, access-controlled engineering repository used to store proprietary technical files related to CMT\'s product development efforts, including all files associated with the NeuroGuide surgical navigation platform. Access to VaultDrive is controlled through individual user accounts with role-based permissions. All file access, download, upload, and administrative events within VaultDrive are recorded in a centralized audit log.

Oshiro\'s VaultDrive account (roshiro@castillomedtech.com) held administrator-level access privileges by virtue of his role as Vice President of Engineering. His account had read and download access to multiple project directories within the NeuroGuide 4.0 project hierarchy, including but not limited to:

> • /NeuroGuide4.0/NeuralPath/
>
> • /NeuroGuide4.0/PrecisionDrive/
>
> • /NeuroGuide4.0/PathPlanner/
>
> • /NeuroGuide4.0/Strategy/

Analysis of the VaultDrive access logs for Oshiro\'s account during the period January 8, 2024, through February 28, 2024, reveals a total of **4,714 file download events** initiated from Oshiro\'s account across these directories. For purposes of this report, this figure is rounded to \"approximately 4,700\" in summary references. Each download event is individually logged with a timestamp, the username of the requesting account, the full file path of the downloaded file, the file size, and the originating IP address. In every instance, the originating IP address for Oshiro\'s download events corresponded to the static internal IP address assigned to Oshiro\'s CMT-issued laptop.

The timestamps of the 4,714 VaultDrive file download events were cross-referenced with the 14 USB connection sessions identified in Section V.B. This cross-referencing confirmed that the VaultDrive downloads align precisely with the periods during which USB drive CX-8827491 was connected to the laptop. No significant VaultDrive download activity was observed outside of these USB connection windows during the January 8--February 28 period. This correlation strongly indicates that the files downloaded from VaultDrive were simultaneously or immediately thereafter copied to the USB drive.

Prior to January 8, 2024, Oshiro\'s typical VaultDrive access pattern involved between 20 and 40 file access events per day on business days, a volume consistent with the day-to-day responsibilities of a Vice President of Engineering who regularly reviews design files, source code, and project documents. The pattern shifted dramatically beginning January 8, 2024, with bulk download operations ranging from approximately 200 to 500 files per session. This shift in access behavior is discussed further in Section V.F below.

**V.E. Attempted Log Deletion --- Anti-Forensic Activity**

On February 27, 2024, at approximately 21:14 CST (9:14 PM), VaultDrive audit logs record that Oshiro\'s account (roshiro@castillomedtech.com) executed a series of administrative commands directed at the VaultDrive audit trail. Specifically, Oshiro used his administrator-level access to execute a \"purge*log\" command targeting his own user access history for the period January 1, 2024, through February 27, 2024. The purge*log command is a system administration function intended for use by VaultDrive system administrators for log maintenance purposes. Oshiro\'s administrator-level account had technical permission to execute this command, although CMT\'s internal policies restrict the use of the purge_log function to the IT department.

The deletion command was partially successful in clearing Oshiro\'s access log entries from the primary VaultDrive audit log database. Following execution of the command, the primary audit log no longer contained records of Oshiro\'s file download events for the targeted date range.

However, the deletion was captured and fully preserved by CMT\'s **VaultDrive Shadow Archive** --- an automated daily backup system that mirrors all VaultDrive audit log entries to a separate, read-only storage server on a nightly basis. The Shadow Archive is managed by CMT\'s IT infrastructure team and operates independently of the primary VaultDrive system. Critically, the Shadow Archive storage server is not accessible to individual user accounts, including accounts with administrator-level privileges such as Oshiro\'s. The Shadow Archive retained a complete, unaltered copy of all VaultDrive access logs for the period under review, including the 4,714 file download events attributable to Oshiro\'s account and the purge_log command itself.

Ashcroft verified the integrity of the Shadow Archive logs by comparing the hash values of daily log snapshots maintained by the Shadow Archive system against the known hash values of the backup files at the time of their creation. This verification confirmed that no tampering or unauthorized modification had occurred to the Shadow Archive data.

The timing and nature of the log deletion attempt are significant. The purge_log command was executed on the evening of February 27, 2024 --- one day before Oshiro\'s final download session on February 28, 2024, and two business days before his last day at CMT on March 1, 2024. The attempt to destroy evidence of the download activity, at a time proximate to Oshiro\'s departure, is consistent with an awareness on Oshiro\'s part that the download activity was unauthorized and an effort to conceal it from CMT. The after-hours timing of the deletion attempt (9:14 PM) further suggests a deliberate effort to avoid detection by CMT IT personnel who would have been absent from the office.

**V.F. Comparison of Download Activity to Baseline**

To assess whether Oshiro\'s January--February 2024 download activity was anomalous relative to his normal usage of VaultDrive, Ashcroft analyzed the complete VaultDrive access log for Oshiro\'s account for the twelve-month baseline period from January 1, 2023, through December 31, 2023.

During this twelve-month baseline period, Oshiro accessed an average of approximately 28 files per business day via VaultDrive, a figure consistent with the expected activity level of a Vice President of Engineering who regularly reviews design files, code repositories, and project documentation in the course of his duties. The maximum single-day file access count during the baseline period was 127 files, recorded on June 14, 2023, a date that corresponds to a documented NeuroGuide 4.0 design review meeting during which multiple team members accessed a high volume of files. The baseline monthly download volume, averaged across the twelve-month period, was approximately 580 files.

In stark contrast, during the period from January 8 through February 28, 2024, Oshiro\'s VaultDrive activity was dramatically elevated. The average number of files downloaded per USB connection session during this period was approximately 336 files, with a peak of approximately 500 files in a single session. In January 2024 alone, Oshiro downloaded approximately 2,100 files from VaultDrive --- an increase of approximately 362% over his average monthly baseline volume. Across the full exfiltration period of approximately seven-and-a-half weeks, the cumulative download count of 4,714 files exceeded Oshiro\'s entire twelve-month baseline total of approximately 6,960 files by a factor of nearly 68% in a fraction of the time.

The access pattern was also qualitatively different from Oshiro\'s baseline behavior. During the baseline period, Oshiro\'s VaultDrive access consisted predominantly of individual file views and occasional small-batch downloads --- patterns consistent with reviewing specific documents or code files as needed for engineering decisions. During the exfiltration period, by contrast, the access consisted of concentrated bulk download batches, with hundreds of files downloaded in succession within the span of a single USB connection session. This pattern is characteristic of data harvesting --- the systematic collection of large volumes of files --- rather than day-to-day engineering work.

Additionally, during the January 8--February 28 exfiltration period, Oshiro accessed VaultDrive directories that he had not accessed at any point during the prior twelve months. These include the /NeuroGuide4.0/Strategy/CompIntel/ directory (competitive intelligence memoranda) and the /NeuroGuide4.0/PathPlanner/TrainingData/ directory (machine-learning training datasets). While Oshiro\'s administrator-level account had technical permission to access these directories, the training data directory would typically be accessed by CMT\'s machine-learning research team rather than the VP of Engineering, and the competitive intelligence directory would typically be accessed by CMT\'s strategy and business development personnel. Oshiro\'s sudden interest in these directories, coinciding with the bulk download activity, further distinguishes the exfiltration-period access from his normal workflow.

The anomalous access pattern commenced on January 8, 2024, a date that falls approximately five weeks after December 3, 2023 --- the date on which, according to information provided to Ashcroft by CMT\'s litigation counsel, Veridian Health Systems CEO Marcus Trent allegedly initiated contact with Oshiro via LinkedIn. Ashcroft has not independently verified the existence or content of this LinkedIn communication; the temporal observation is noted here based on information provided by counsel and is offered for context rather than as an independent forensic finding. Nevertheless, the coincidence in timing is consistent with the theory that Oshiro commenced data harvesting in the wake of a recruitment overture from a competitor.

Access logs for the evidence storage room are maintained by Ashcroft and are available for review. All forensic images and original evidence items remain available for inspection by opposing counsel or the Court upon appropriate notice.

**[VII. OPINIONS AND CONCLUSIONS]{.underline}**

Based on the forensic analysis described in this report, and drawing upon my education, training, and fourteen years of professional experience in the field of digital forensics, I offer the following opinions to a reasonable degree of certainty within the field of digital forensics:

**Opinion 1.** Ryan Oshiro systematically downloaded approximately 4,700 files, totaling approximately 38.4 GB of data, from CMT\'s VaultDrive secure engineering repository to a personal Corsair USB portable drive bearing serial number CX-8827491 between January 8, 2024, and February 28, 2024. The downloads occurred across fourteen discrete sessions corresponding to the fourteen dates on which the USB drive was connected to Oshiro\'s CMT-issued laptop.

**Opinion 2.** The downloaded files encompass CMT\'s core proprietary technologies for the NeuroGuide 4.0 platform, specifically: (a) the complete source code repository for the NeuralPath Algorithm (approximately 2,100 files, 14.6 GB); (b) full CAD schematics and mechanical design files for the PrecisionDrive System (approximately 1,200 files, 8.2 GB); (c) training datasets, model weights, and configuration files for the PathPlanner Module (approximately 800 files, 12.1 GB); and (d) internal R&D roadmap documents, competitive intelligence memoranda, and patent strategy documents (approximately 600 files, 3.5 GB). These categories correspond to all three trade secrets identified by CMT in this matter, together with strategic business documents that would inform the commercial exploitation of those technologies.

**Opinion 3.** The download pattern is consistent with deliberate, systematic exfiltration of proprietary data and is inconsistent with ordinary work-related access. The indicators of deliberate exfiltration include: (a) the use of a personal USB drive never previously connected to the CMT laptop; (b) bulk download operations of 200--500 files per session, far exceeding Oshiro\'s baseline access of 20--40 files per day; (c) access to VaultDrive directories outside Oshiro\'s normal workflow, including machine-learning training data and competitive intelligence; (d) the commencement of the download activity approximately five weeks after the alleged recruitment contact from a competitor; and (e) the continuation of download activity through the date of resignation and beyond, including after-hours sessions.

**Opinion 4.** Oshiro\'s attempted deletion of VaultDrive access logs on February 27, 2024, using the purge_log administrative command, constitutes evidence of an effort to conceal the data exfiltration activity and demonstrates consciousness of wrongdoing. The deletion was undertaken after business hours, targeted specifically the date range encompassing the download activity, and was executed shortly before Oshiro\'s departure from CMT.

**Opinion 5.** USB drive CX-8827491 was not returned to CMT and is presumed to remain in Oshiro\'s possession or to have been transferred to another party. Ashcroft was not engaged to and did not examine any devices, systems, or networks belonging to Veridian Health Systems, LLC, and accordingly cannot opine on whether USB drive CX-8827491 was ever connected to any Veridian device or system. However, the comprehensive nature of the exfiltrated data set --- encompassing all technical specifications, source code, training data, model weights, and strategic planning documents necessary to replicate the NeuroGuide 4.0 platform --- means that possession of and access to the contents of this USB drive would provide a complete technical blueprint for CMT\'s flagship product development program. Any entity or individual who subsequently accessed, copied, or received files from this USB drive would thereby have obtained CMT\'s proprietary and confidential information in its entirety.

**[VIII. LIMITATIONS]{.underline}**

The findings and opinions set forth in this report are subject to the following limitations:

This report is based solely on the evidence provided to Ashcroft by CMT and its litigation counsel, Hargrove, Pell & Sutton LLP. Ashcroft has relied upon the representations of CMT and its counsel regarding the provenance and completeness of the evidence provided.

Ashcroft did not have access to any devices, systems, accounts, or networks belonging to Veridian Health Systems, LLC, to Ryan Oshiro personally, or to any other third party. The investigation was limited to CMT\'s systems and data.

Ashcroft was not able to examine USB drive CX-8827491 itself, as that device was not in CMT\'s possession at any point during this investigation. The identification of the USB drive and the determination of the files transferred to it are based on digital artifacts recovered from Oshiro\'s CMT-issued laptop (including Windows Registry entries, SetupAPI logs, Event Logs, Shellbags, Jump Lists, and LNK files) and VaultDrive access logs. While these artifacts are highly reliable indicators of USB device identity and file transfer activity, direct examination of the USB drive itself would provide additional confirmation.

Ashcroft has not independently verified the content, timing, or nature of the alleged LinkedIn communication between Marcus Trent and Ryan Oshiro on December 3, 2023. The temporal correlation noted in Section V.F of this report is based on information provided by CMT\'s counsel and is offered for contextual purposes. It is not an independent forensic finding.

The opinions expressed in this report are based on reasonable certainty within the field of digital forensics. They reflect the application of generally accepted forensic methodologies to the available evidence.

Ashcroft reserves the right to supplement or amend this report should additional evidence or information become available, including but not limited to the USB drive itself, Oshiro\'s personal devices, or any data from Veridian Health Systems\' systems.

SynapticEdge is Veridian\'s most advanced surgical robotics system, designed to redefine precision in neurosurgical and orthopedic procedures. The platform represents the culmination of Veridian\'s expanded investment in next-generation surgical technology. Veridian, which reported approximately \$94 million in revenue in 2023 and employs approximately 420 professionals across its engineering, regulatory, and commercial teams, developed SynapticEdge as the centerpiece of its strategic growth initiative in AI-enhanced robotic surgery.

\"SynapticEdge is a milestone not only for Veridian but for the entire surgical robotics industry,\" said **Marcus Trent, Chief Executive Officer of Veridian Health Systems, LLC**. \"We have been relentless in assembling world-class talent and investing in the technologies that will define the next era of surgical precision. Since joining Veridian as our Senior Vice President of Product Development, Ryan Oshiro has brought unparalleled expertise and vision to our engineering organization. His leadership has been instrumental in bringing SynapticEdge to market. This platform reflects our commitment to delivering transformative solutions that improve patient outcomes and empower the surgeons who rely on our systems every day.\"

The SynapticEdge platform incorporates five key technical capabilities that Veridian believes differentiate it from existing surgical robotics systems:

> **Real-Time Neural Mapping Overlay.** SynapticEdge\'s proprietary real-time neural mapping overlay delivers sub-12ms latency for intraoperative visualization, enabling surgeons to navigate neural structures with unprecedented clarity and confidence during complex cranial and spinal procedures.
>
> **Harmonic Micro-Actuation System.** The platform features a harmonic drive micro-actuation system achieving sub-millimeter robotic arm control for delicate neurosurgical and spinal procedures, providing the fine-grained motor precision required for operations in anatomically constrained surgical corridors.
>
> **AI-Powered Surgical Pathway Planning.** SynapticEdge integrates an advanced machine-learning-based surgical planning module that analyzes preoperative imaging data to generate optimized surgical pathways, reducing operative variability and supporting consistent procedural outcomes across diverse patient anatomies.
>
> **Surgeon Calibration Workflow.** An intuitive surgeon calibration interface adapts to individual surgeon preferences and ergonomic profiles in under 90 seconds, minimizing setup time and enabling seamless integration into existing operating room workflows.
>
> **Predictive Error-Correction Protocol.** A proprietary predictive error-correction protocol provides real-time robotic arm drift compensation, ensuring sustained precision throughout extended procedures and reducing the risk of cumulative positional deviation during multi-hour surgeries.

\"SynapticEdge represents a paradigm shift in how surgeons interact with robotic systems during complex procedures,\" said **Ryan Oshiro, Senior Vice President of Product Development, Veridian Health Systems**. \"Drawing on years of experience in surgical robotics, I am proud of what our team has accomplished and believe this platform will set a new standard for precision and safety in the operating room.
