**[II. EXPERT QUALIFICATIONS AND BACKGROUND]{.underline}**

My name is Dr. Lena Yilmaz. I hold a Ph.D. in Biomedical Engineering from the Caldwell Institute of Technology, where my doctoral research focused on real-time feedback control systems for robotic surgical instruments. I am currently a tenured Professor of Biomedical Engineering at Kerrigan University, where I have served on the faculty for approximately fifteen years. I was promoted to full Professor in 2017 and currently direct the Advanced Surgical Robotics Laboratory at Kerrigan University, which employs twelve graduate researchers and three post-doctoral fellows.

My research specialization encompasses surgical robotics, real-time intraoperative imaging systems, robotic micro-actuation technologies, and machine-learning applications in surgical planning. Over the course of my academic career, I have published over eighty peer-reviewed articles in leading journals in these fields, including the *Journal of Surgical Robotics*, the *IEEE Transactions on Biomedical Engineering*, *Annals of Biomedical Engineering*, and *Medical Image Analysis*. I have also presented my research at over forty-five national and international conferences, including the International Surgical Robotics Conference, the IEEE International Conference on Robotics and Automation, and the Annual Meeting of the Society for Medical Innovation and Technology.

I hold three United States patents related to robotic surgical guidance systems, specifically U.S. Patent Nos. 9,814,227; 10,342,615; and 11,027,488, each of which relates to aspects of real-time feedback and control in robotic surgical applications.

In addition to my academic work, I have served as a technical consultant to multiple medical device manufacturers on matters involving the design, validation, and regulatory review of surgical robotics platforms. I have been retained as a testifying expert in four prior trade secret and patent disputes involving surgical robotics technology, in each case providing opinions regarding the technical characteristics of the systems at issue, the state of the art in the relevant field, and the plausibility of independent development. In two of those matters, I testified at trial, and in the remaining two, I provided deposition testimony.

I am being compensated at a rate of \$650 per hour for my work in this matter. My compensation is not contingent upon the outcome of this litigation, and my opinions are based solely on my independent professional judgment and the materials I have reviewed.

A full curriculum vitae setting forth my educational background, professional experience, publications, patents, and prior testimony history is attached to this report as **Exhibit A**.

**[III. SCOPE OF ENGAGEMENT AND MATERIALS REVIEWED]{.underline}**

**III.A. Scope of Engagement**

I was retained by the law firm of Hargrove, Pell & Sutton LLP on behalf of Plaintiff Castillo Medical Technologies, Inc. (\"CMT\") to provide preliminary expert opinions regarding the following issues:

1\. Whether Veridian Health Systems, LLC\'s (\"Veridian\") SynapticEdge surgical robotics platform incorporates technology that is substantially similar to or derived from CMT\'s proprietary trade secrets, specifically: (a) the NeuralPath Algorithm, (b) the PrecisionDrive System, and (c) the PathPlanner Module.

2\. Whether the technical overlaps between SynapticEdge and CMT\'s NeuroGuide 4.0 platform could plausibly result from independent parallel development, given the known development timeline and circumstances.

This is a preliminary report, prepared in connection with the preliminary injunction hearing currently scheduled before this Court. I reserve the right to supplement or amend my opinions following review of additional discovery materials, including but not limited to Veridian\'s source code for SynapticEdge, Veridian\'s internal design and development documentation, version control history, and deposition testimony of relevant witnesses. My analysis at this stage is based on publicly available information about SynapticEdge, the forensic investigation report documenting Defendant Oshiro\'s file downloads, and my review of CMT\'s NeuroGuide 4.0 technology under the terms of a protective order. Throughout this report, I describe CMT\'s trade secrets at a functional and architectural level, identifying their observable performance characteristics and general capabilities, rather than disclosing the underlying source code, algorithmic details, design parameters, or other proprietary technical specifications that constitute the confidential substance of CMT\'s trade secrets.

**III.B. Materials Reviewed**

In preparing this preliminary report, I reviewed the following materials:

1\. First Amended Complaint, *Castillo Medical Technologies, Inc. v. Oshiro et al.*, Case No. 1:24-cv-03841-RLH (W.D. Tex.), filed July 22, 2024.

2\. Confidentiality, Non-Disclosure, and Invention Assignment Agreement between CMT and Ryan Oshiro, dated March 5, 2018.

3\. Ashcroft Digital Forensics, LLC, Forensic Investigation Report, dated May 15, 2024, documenting file downloads from CMT\'s VaultDrive secure engineering repository to a USB drive (serial number CX-8827491) between January 8 and February 28, 2024, comprising approximately 4,700 files totaling 38.4 GB, including approximately 1,200 CAD schematics for the PrecisionDrive System, approximately 2,100 source code files for the NeuralPath Algorithm, approximately 800 PathPlanner training datasets and model weights, and approximately 600 internal R&D roadmap documents and competitive intelligence memoranda.

4\. Veridian Health Systems, LLC, press release dated June 11, 2024, announcing the SynapticEdge surgical robotics platform at the International Surgical Robotics Conference in Chicago, Illinois.

5\. Publicly available marketing materials and technical specifications for the SynapticEdge platform, as presented at the International Surgical Robotics Conference on June 11, 2024.

6\. CMT internal technical documentation regarding the NeuroGuide 4.0 platform, including architecture documents, engineering specifications, and testing data. This documentation was reviewed under the terms of a protective order to be entered by the Court, and its specific contents are not disclosed in this report in order to preserve the confidentiality of CMT\'s trade secrets.

7\. Video recording of the SynapticEdge demonstration at the International Surgical Robotics Conference, Chicago, Illinois, June 11, 2024 (approximately 47 minutes in duration).

8\. Published academic literature and publicly available patent filings relating to surgical robotics, neural mapping, micro-actuation, and surgical planning artificial intelligence. A list of specific references is provided in **Exhibit B**.

9\. CMT\'s twenty-three (23) active U.S. patents and fourteen (14) pending patent applications related to real-time neural mapping and robotic arm micro-actuation, reviewed for the purpose of distinguishing publicly disclosed technology from the trade secrets at issue in this litigation.

10\. Order Granting in Part Plaintiff\'s Motion for Temporary Restraining Order, dated August 2, 2024 (Hon. Rebecca L. Hightower).

11\. Notes from my interviews with three CMT engineering personnel who attended the SynapticEdge presentation on June 11, 2024, conducted on July 29 and July 30, 2024.

**[IV. BACKGROUND: CMT\'S NEUROGUIDE 4.0 PLATFORM AND TRADE SECRETS AT ISSUE]{.underline}**

**IV.A. Overview of CMT and NeuroGuide**

Castillo Medical Technologies, Inc. is a Delaware corporation headquartered at 4200 Lakeshore Parkway, Suite 800, Austin, Texas 78746. CMT was founded in 2011 by Dr. Elena Castillo, a neurosurgeon and biomedical engineer, with the mission of developing advanced robotic systems to improve the safety and precision of neurosurgical procedures. Since its founding, CMT has grown into a leading developer and manufacturer of proprietary surgical robotic systems.

CMT\'s flagship product line is the NeuroGuide platform, an AI-assisted neurosurgical robot currently deployed in over 340 hospitals across North America. The NeuroGuide platform integrates real-time intraoperative imaging, robotic instrument control, and machine-learning-based surgical planning to assist neurosurgeons in performing complex procedures with greater precision and safety. CMT reported 2023 revenue of approximately \$287 million and currently employs approximately 1,100 individuals.

The current generation of the platform, NeuroGuide 4.0, represents the culmination of years of intensive research and development. CMT invested approximately \$46.3 million in R&D for NeuroGuide 4.0 between 2021 and 2023, including \$18.7 million in 2021, \$14.9 million in 2022, and \$12.7 million in 2023. This investment supported the development and refinement of the three proprietary technologies that are at the center of this dispute.

Defendant Ryan Oshiro served as Vice President of Engineering at CMT from March 5, 2018, to March 1, 2024. In that capacity, Oshiro led the NeuroGuide 4.0 development team, reporting directly to CMT\'s Chief Technology Officer, Dr. Priya Ramaswamy. Oshiro had comprehensive access to all aspects of the NeuroGuide 4.0 platform, including the three trade secrets described below.

**IV.B. The Three Trade Secrets at Issue**

CMT\'s Complaint identifies three proprietary technologies as the trade secrets at issue in this litigation. I describe each below at a functional and architectural level, focusing on what these technologies do and their observable performance characteristics, rather than disclosing the underlying source code, specific algorithmic methods, design parameters, or other proprietary details that constitute the confidential substance of these trade secrets. This approach is intentional: it allows me to provide meaningful analysis while preserving the confidentiality of CMT\'s proprietary information.

**1. NeuralPath Algorithm.** The NeuralPath Algorithm is a proprietary real-time intraoperative neural mapping algorithm developed by CMT over a period of approximately three years. It enables surgeons to visualize neural pathway activity in real time during neurosurgical procedures, providing a dynamic overlay of neural mapping data on the surgical field. A key observable performance characteristic of the NeuralPath Algorithm is its sub-12 millisecond latency for the neural mapping overlay---meaning that the visual representation of neural pathway activity is updated within 12 milliseconds of the underlying neural signal being detected. Achieving this latency benchmark requires a specific and non-obvious combination of signal processing techniques, data pipeline architecture, and GPU optimization strategies. The algorithm\'s internal architecture, specific computational methods, signal processing pipeline, and source code are trade secrets that have not been publicly disclosed.

**2. PrecisionDrive System.** The PrecisionDrive System is a novel micro-actuation system for robotic arm control that enables sub-millimeter precision in neurosurgical instrument positioning. The system employs a proprietary harmonic drive configuration---a type of gear mechanism used in precision robotics---that has been specifically designed and optimized for the unique demands of neurosurgical applications. The specific design parameters of the harmonic drive configuration, including the gear ratio, wave generator design, flex spline characteristics, control algorithms, and CAD specifications, are trade secrets that have not been publicly disclosed.

**3. PathPlanner Module.** The PathPlanner Module is a machine-learning-based surgical planning module that uses proprietary training datasets and model weights to predict optimal surgical pathways for individual patients based on pre-operative imaging and clinical data. CMT curated the training data over several years through clinical partnerships with hospitals using the NeuroGuide platform. The dataset structure, training methodology, model architecture, hyperparameters, and trained model weights are trade secrets that have not been publicly disclosed.

It is important to note that these three trade secrets are distinct from, and not fully disclosed by, CMT\'s twenty-three active U.S. patents and fourteen pending patent applications. I reviewed those patents and applications in connection with this engagement. While CMT\'s patent portfolio covers various aspects of the broader NeuroGuide technology, the patents describe the technology at a level of generality that does not disclose the specific internal implementations described above. The specific NeuralPath Algorithm code and signal processing pipeline, the specific PrecisionDrive harmonic drive configuration and design parameters, and the specific PathPlanner training datasets and model weights remain confidential trade secrets that are not ascertainable from CMT\'s public patent filings.

**[V. BACKGROUND: VERIDIAN\'S SYNAPTICEDGE PLATFORM]{.underline}**

Veridian Health Systems, LLC is a Virginia limited liability company headquartered at 11700 Commerce Park Drive, Suite 300, Reston, Virginia 20191. Veridian was founded in 2016 and develops surgical robotics systems and AI-guided surgical planning software. Veridian reported 2023 revenue of approximately \$94 million and employs approximately 420 individuals. Prior to 2024, Veridian\'s publicly known product line focused on orthopedic surgical robotics and did not include neurosurgical applications.

On March 18, 2024, Veridian publicly announced the hiring of Ryan Oshiro as its Senior Vice President of Product Development. This announcement came only seventeen days after Oshiro\'s last day of employment at CMT on March 1, 2024. The press release announcing Oshiro\'s hiring described him as a \"visionary leader in surgical robotics\" and stated that he would \"spearhead the development of Veridian\'s next-generation surgical platform.\"

On June 11, 2024, Veridian announced SynapticEdge at the International Surgical Robotics Conference in Chicago, Illinois. The announcement came approximately eighty-five days after Oshiro\'s publicly announced start date at Veridian. The SynapticEdge platform was presented as Veridian\'s entry into the neurosurgical robotics market---a field in which, as noted above, Veridian had no publicly known prior presence.

The Veridian press release dated June 11, 2024, credited Oshiro as leading the SynapticEdge development effort and highlighted several technical features of the platform, including \"sub-12ms neural mapping overlay,\" \"harmonic micro-actuation for sub-millimeter surgical precision,\" and \"AI-powered surgical pathway planning.\" The press release also noted a \$60 million Series C investment in Veridian by Thorncastle Ventures, described as funding for the commercialization of SynapticEdge.

The timeline described above is, in my professional opinion, extraordinarily compressed. As I discuss in detail in the following sections of this report, developing the technical capabilities demonstrated by SynapticEdge would typically require many months to years of dedicated engineering effort. The approximately eighty-five-day window between Oshiro\'s arrival at Veridian and the public demonstration of SynapticEdge raises significant questions about whether the platform could have been developed independently in that timeframe.

**[VI. TECHNICAL ANALYSIS: FIVE AREAS OF SUBSTANTIAL OVERLAP]{.underline}**

**VI.A. Overview of Methodology**

My technical analysis in this preliminary report is based on a comparison of publicly available information about SynapticEdge---drawn from Veridian\'s press release, conference presentation materials, and the video recording of the June 11, 2024 demonstration---with my understanding of CMT\'s NeuroGuide 4.0 architecture, which I reviewed under the terms of a protective order. I supplemented this comparison with the findings of the Ashcroft Digital Forensics report, which identifies the categories and quantities of files downloaded by Oshiro from CMT\'s VaultDrive repository.

In addition, I conducted a comprehensive review of published academic literature and publicly available patent filings in the fields of surgical robotics, neural mapping, robotic micro-actuation, and surgical planning artificial intelligence. The purpose of this literature review was to assess the current state of the art and to evaluate whether the specific features overlapping between NeuroGuide 4.0 and SynapticEdge could plausibly have been developed independently based on publicly available knowledge and technology.

I also considered the development timeline and resource constraints at Veridian, including the approximately eighty-five-day period between Oshiro\'s arrival and the SynapticEdge announcement, Veridian\'s lack of prior neurosurgical robotics products, and the company\'s reported size and resources.

I wish to note that this is a preliminary analysis. Full technical analysis, including detailed code-level and design-level comparisons, will be provided in a supplemental report after I have had the opportunity to review Veridian\'s source code, design documents, version control history, and related discovery materials. The opinions expressed in this report are based on the publicly observable features and architectural patterns identified to date.

**VI.B. Overlap #1 --- Real-Time Neural Mapping Overlay (NeuralPath Algorithm)**

SynapticEdge\'s neural mapping overlay feature, as demonstrated at the International Surgical Robotics Conference on June 11, 2024, displays real-time neural pathway activity during simulated neurosurgical procedures. Veridian\'s own press release states that the system achieves a \"sub-12ms neural mapping overlay.\" I observed this feature in operation during my review of the forty-seven-minute demonstration video.

CMT\'s NeuralPath Algorithm achieves the identical sub-12 millisecond latency specification for its neural mapping overlay. This is a highly significant point. Based on my review of published academic literature, the current state of the art for comparable real-time neural mapping systems in surgical robotics is in the range of 25 to 40 milliseconds of latency. Achieving sub-12 millisecond latency requires a specific and non-obvious combination of signal processing techniques, data pipeline architecture, and GPU optimization---none of which, in the specific combination required, has been published in the open literature. CMT\'s achievement of this benchmark was the result of approximately three years of dedicated R&D and tens of millions of dollars of investment.

The visual presentation of the neural mapping overlay in SynapticEdge also warrants comment. CMT engineers who attended the June 11, 2024 demonstration, and whom I subsequently interviewed, identified the SynapticEdge neural mapping interface layout and color-mapping scheme as substantially similar to NeuroGuide 4.0\'s proprietary interface. While I have not yet been able to conduct a pixel-level comparison, the overall visual similarity observed in the demonstration video is consistent with the CMT engineers\' observations.

The probability that Veridian independently achieved the identical sub-12 millisecond latency specification using a completely different underlying technical approach is low. This is not a standard industry benchmark that multiple developers would naturally converge upon; it is a specific performance target that CMT achieved through proprietary engineering decisions. It is also significant that the NeuralPath Algorithm\'s source code comprised approximately 2,100 of the approximately 4,700 files---nearly 45% of the total---downloaded by Oshiro to USB drive CX-8827491 during the period of January 8 through February 28, 2024.

**VI.C. Overlap #2 --- Micro-Actuation Using Harmonic Drive Configuration (PrecisionDrive System)**

SynapticEdge employs a micro-actuation system for robotic arm control that uses what Veridian\'s press release describes as \"harmonic micro-actuation for sub-millimeter surgical precision.\" I observed the operation of this micro-actuation system during the conference demonstration video.

CMT\'s PrecisionDrive System uses a proprietary harmonic drive configuration that enables sub-millimeter robotic arm control in neurosurgical procedures. While harmonic drives are a known category of technology in the broader robotics field---used in applications ranging from industrial automation to aerospace---their application to surgical micro-actuation for sub-millimeter precision in neurosurgery is highly specialized. The specific configuration of a harmonic drive for this application, including the gear ratio, wave generator design, and flex spline characteristics, involves numerous interdependent design choices that are not dictated by the general principles of harmonic drive technology.

My review of published literature identified only a small number of research groups worldwide working on harmonic drive configurations for surgical robotics applications, and none had published results demonstrating the precision levels exhibited by both NeuroGuide 4.0 and SynapticEdge. SynapticEdge\'s demonstrated micro-actuation performance characteristics---including the range of motion, speed of actuation, and apparent precision in the simulated procedures shown during the conference demonstration---appear to closely mirror the capabilities of CMT\'s PrecisionDrive System.

It is further significant that the 1,200 CAD schematics for the PrecisionDrive System were among the files downloaded by Oshiro to USB drive CX-8827491. Designing, prototyping, and testing a novel harmonic drive configuration for surgical robotics applications typically requires eighteen to thirty-six months of dedicated engineering effort. This timeline reflects the need for iterative design, custom manufacturing of precision components, extensive bench testing, and validation against surgical performance requirements. The approximately eighty-five-day window between Oshiro\'s start date at Veridian and the SynapticEdge announcement is wholly insufficient for the independent development of such a system from scratch.

**VI.D. Overlap #3 --- Machine-Learning Surgical Planning Module (PathPlanner Module)**

SynapticEdge includes what Veridian\'s press release describes as \"AI-powered surgical pathway planning.\" I observed this feature during the June 11, 2024 demonstration, where the system processed simulated patient imaging data and generated proposed surgical pathways for the operator\'s review.

CMT\'s PathPlanner Module is a machine-learning-based surgical planning module that uses proprietary training datasets and model weights to predict optimal surgical pathways. The training data was curated over several years from clinical partnerships with hospitals using the NeuroGuide platform. The accumulation of this dataset---comprising de-identified patient imaging data, intraoperative recordings, and surgical outcome data---represents a substantial investment of time, resources, and clinical collaboration.

During my review of the SynapticEdge demonstration, I observed that the surgical planning interface processes patient imaging data in a workflow and with output visualizations that are structurally similar to PathPlanner\'s documented workflow. Specifically, the sequential steps in SynapticEdge\'s planning workflow---image ingestion, anatomical segmentation, pathway generation, risk scoring, and surgeon review---follow the same sequence as PathPlanner\'s documented workflow. While certain individual steps in this sequence are conceptually known in the field (e.g., anatomical segmentation is a standard image processing task), the specific combination of steps, the order in which they are presented to the surgeon, and the nature of the output visualizations reflect proprietary design choices made by CMT during the development of PathPlanner.

Training an effective machine-learning model for surgical pathway planning requires three critical components: (a) a large, curated dataset of surgical outcomes and associated imaging data; (b) substantial computational resources for model training and optimization; and (c) iterative refinement through clinical validation. CMT invested approximately \$12.7 million in 2023 alone on R&D activities that included PathPlanner development and refinement. The 800 PathPlanner training datasets and model weights downloaded by Oshiro to USB drive CX-8827491 represent years of accumulated clinical data. If these files were used to train or bootstrap SynapticEdge\'s machine-learning model, it would readily explain how Veridian achieved a functional surgical planning module in such a compressed timeframe---a feat that would otherwise require years of data collection and model development.

I note that the dataset structure observable in SynapticEdge\'s demonstration outputs appears consistent with what I understand of PathPlanner\'s dataset architecture, though full confirmation of this observation will require source code and data review during the discovery phase.

**VI.E. Overlap #4 --- Identical Surgeon Calibration User-Interface Workflow**

CMT engineers who attended the SynapticEdge demonstration on June 11, 2024, identified that SynapticEdge\'s surgeon calibration workflow is substantially identical to the calibration workflow in NeuroGuide 4.0. I confirmed this observation through my own comparative review of the SynapticEdge demonstration video and NeuroGuide 4.0\'s calibration interface.

The calibration workflow in NeuroGuide 4.0 involves a specific multi-step sequence that CMT developed through extensive usability testing and iterative design over more than two years. While the individual conceptual steps of surgical system calibration---such as hand registration, instrument calibration, and range-of-motion verification---are generally known in the field, the specific sequence of these steps, the user-interface elements used to implement each step, the visual feedback mechanisms, and the error-handling protocols in SynapticEdge match NeuroGuide 4.0\'s implementation in ways that go well beyond what would be expected from independent design.

In my review of the demonstration video, I identified at least seven specific user-interface elements that appear to be replicated between the two platforms, including the spatial layout of calibration panels on the display, the use of color-coded feedback indicators for calibration status, and the sequencing and gating of verification steps. The convergence on all seven of these design elements in a single workflow is inconsistent with independent development, as user-interface design for surgical systems is highly customized and reflects numerous independent design decisions at each stage.

The overlap in calibration workflow design suggests access to CMT\'s internal design documentation. Such documentation would be included in the approximately 600 internal R&D roadmap documents and competitive intelligence memoranda that the Ashcroft forensic investigation confirmed were downloaded by Oshiro.

**VI.F. Overlap #5 --- Shared Error-Correction Protocol for Robotic Arm Drift**

During the SynapticEdge demonstration, the system exhibited an error-correction response to simulated robotic arm drift that CMT engineers recognized as functionally identical to NeuroGuide 4.0\'s proprietary drift-correction protocol. I reviewed the relevant segment of the demonstration video multiple times and concur with this assessment based on the observable characteristics of the correction.

Robotic arm drift---the gradual deviation of a robotic surgical instrument from its programmed trajectory due to mechanical tolerances, thermal effects, or other factors---is a well-known challenge in surgical robotics. The published literature describes multiple approaches to drift correction, including PID-based correction loops, sensor fusion techniques, and predictive modeling algorithms. CMT\'s NeuroGuide 4.0 employs a proprietary multi-layered error-correction protocol that combines elements of multiple correction approaches in a specific, non-obvious sequence and with particular tuning characteristics.

The error-correction response observed in SynapticEdge closely matched NeuroGuide 4.0\'s behavior in three measurable dimensions: the correction latency (the time delay between drift detection and initiation of correction), the magnitude of the compensatory adjustment, and the smoothness of the correction curve (i.e., the absence of oscillation or overcorrection). These response characteristics are directly determined by the underlying correction algorithms and their tuning parameters, which are part of CMT\'s trade secrets. The probability that Veridian independently arrived at the same combination of correction techniques with the same tuning characteristics, producing observably identical correction behavior, is low.

This error-correction protocol would be embedded in the NeuralPath Algorithm source code (which governs the real-time control loop) and the PrecisionDrive control software (which executes the physical correction), both of which were among the categories of files downloaded by Oshiro.

**[VII. INDEPENDENT DEVELOPMENT ANALYSIS]{.underline}**

**VII.A. Probability Assessment**

Based on my analysis of the five areas of substantial overlap described above, it is my professional opinion that the probability of independent parallel development of all five overlapping features is extraordinarily low.

I acknowledge that any single one of the five overlaps identified above, considered in isolation, might conceivably be attributed to independent development or convergent engineering---though even individually, each overlap is noteworthy given the specificity of the features involved. However, the combination of all five overlapping features---each involving specific, non-obvious design choices that match CMT\'s proprietary implementations---is both statistically and practically implausible as a coincidence.

My reasoning is based on five principal factors:

1\. **Specificity.** The overlaps identified in this report are not at the level of general conceptual similarity (e.g., \"both systems use neural mapping\" or \"both systems use harmonic drives\"). Rather, the overlaps exist at the level of specific implementation details: identical latency specifications, identical user-interface workflows, identical error-correction behavior, and structurally similar machine-learning workflows. These specific implementation details reflect proprietary engineering decisions made by CMT that are not dictated by the general state of the art.

2\. **Multiplicity.** The existence of five independent instances of convergence compounds the improbability. Even if the probability of coincidental convergence on any single feature were non-trivial, the joint probability of convergence on all five features simultaneously is dramatically lower.

3\. **Timeline.** SynapticEdge was publicly announced approximately eighty-five days after Oshiro joined Veridian. As discussed in each overlap analysis above, developing any one of these five features independently would typically require many months to years of dedicated engineering effort. Developing all five in approximately eighty-five days is not credible in the absence of prior access to CMT\'s proprietary technology.

4\. **Personnel.** Oshiro, who led the NeuroGuide 4.0 development team and had comprehensive access to all three trade secrets at issue, joined Veridian immediately before SynapticEdge\'s announcement. In addition, three CMT engineers who worked under Oshiro on the NeuroGuide 4.0 development team also joined Veridian between May and July 2024: Dr. Kenji Furukawa (Senior Algorithm Engineer, specializing in the NeuralPath Algorithm), Samantha Briggs (Lead Mechanical Engineer, specializing in the PrecisionDrive System), and David Moreno (Machine Learning Researcher, specializing in the PathPlanner Module). The departure of these four individuals---each a specialist in one or more of the three trade secrets---to Veridian during the same period is a significant circumstantial factor.

5\. **File Downloads.** The Ashcroft Digital Forensics report documents that Oshiro downloaded approximately 4,700 files (38.4 GB) from CMT\'s VaultDrive secure engineering repository to USB drive CX-8827491 between January 8 and February 28, 2024---the final two months of his employment at CMT. These files encompass all three trade secret categories: NeuralPath Algorithm source code, PrecisionDrive CAD schematics, and PathPlanner training datasets and model weights, as well as internal R&D roadmap documents.

Considering these five factors in combination, I conclude that the most plausible technical explanation for the five areas of substantial overlap between SynapticEdge and NeuroGuide 4.0 is that SynapticEdge\'s development incorporated CMT\'s proprietary trade secret technology, either directly or derivatively.

**VII.B. State-of-the-Art Comparison**

To further assess the plausibility of independent development, I surveyed the published academic literature and publicly available patent filings in the fields of surgical robotics, neural mapping, micro-actuation, and surgical planning artificial intelligence. The specific references reviewed are listed in **Exhibit B**.

My survey confirmed that no publicly available technology, published research, or combination thereof achieves the specific combination of performance characteristics demonstrated by both NeuroGuide 4.0 and SynapticEdge. Specifically, no published work demonstrates the combination of (a) sub-12 millisecond neural mapping overlay latency, (b) sub-millimeter harmonic micro-actuation for neurosurgical applications, (c) machine-learning-based surgical pathway planning with the specific workflow sequence observed in both systems, (d) the specific calibration interface workflow, and (e) the specific error-correction behavior for robotic arm drift.

Several academic research groups and commercial entities are working on individual components of this technology. For example, researchers at various institutions have published work on real-time neural mapping, though at substantially higher latency levels (25--40 ms). Harmonic drives are the subject of active research for non-surgical and general surgical robotics applications, but published work does not address the specific neurosurgical micro-actuation requirements at issue. Machine-learning surgical planning is an active area of research but remains at earlier stages of development, with no published system demonstrating the maturity of the workflow observed in both NeuroGuide 4.0 and SynapticEdge. None of these published works, individually or in combination, would serve as a blueprint for independently developing the five overlapping features identified in this report.

Finally, I note that CMT\'s own twenty-three active U.S. patents and fourteen pending patent applications, while disclosing certain aspects of the broader NeuroGuide technology to the public, do not disclose the specific trade secrets at issue. The patents describe the technology at a level of generality that does not reveal the internal NeuralPath Algorithm code and pipeline, the specific PrecisionDrive harmonic drive configuration and parameters, or the PathPlanner training datasets and model weights. Accordingly, CMT\'s patent portfolio could not have served as a public source from which Veridian independently derived the features that overlap with CMT\'s trade secrets.

**[VIII. OPINIONS AND CONCLUSIONS]{.underline}**

Based on my qualifications, experience, review of the materials identified in Section III, and the analysis set forth in this report, I hold the following preliminary opinions to a reasonable degree of scientific and engineering certainty:

1\. SynapticEdge exhibits five specific areas of substantial technical overlap with CMT\'s NeuroGuide 4.0 platform, each involving proprietary features that constitute trade secrets of CMT: (a) real-time neural mapping overlay with sub-12 millisecond latency; (b) micro-actuation using a harmonic drive configuration for sub-millimeter precision; (c) machine-learning-based surgical pathway planning with a structurally similar workflow; (d) a substantially identical surgeon calibration user-interface workflow; and (e) a functionally identical error-correction protocol for robotic arm drift.

2\. The five overlapping features correspond to the three identified CMT trade secrets as follows: the NeuralPath Algorithm is implicated by Overlaps #1 and #5; the PrecisionDrive System is implicated by Overlaps #2 and #5; and the PathPlanner Module is implicated by Overlap #3. The surgeon calibration workflow overlap (Overlap #4) implicates proprietary design elements documented in CMT\'s internal R&D materials across all three trade secret categories.

3\. The overlaps identified in this report are at the level of specific implementation details---including identical performance specifications, identical user-interface designs, and identical observable system behaviors---rather than merely general conceptual approaches. This level of specificity indicates derivation from CMT\'s technology rather than independent convergent development.

4\. The probability that Veridian independently developed all five overlapping features is extraordinarily low, considering: (a) the specificity of the overlaps; (b) the multiplicity of overlapping features across five distinct technical areas; (c) the compressed development timeline of approximately eighty-five days between Oshiro\'s arrival at Veridian and the public announcement of SynapticEdge; (d) the movement of four CMT personnel with specialized knowledge of CMT\'s trade secrets---Ryan Oshiro, Dr. Kenji Furukawa, Samantha Briggs, and David Moreno---to Veridian during the relevant period; and (e) the forensic evidence documenting Oshiro\'s download of approximately 4,700 CMT files totaling 38.4 GB, encompassing all three trade secret categories, to USB drive CX-8827491 during the final two months of his employment at CMT.

5\. The most plausible technical explanation for the observed overlaps is that SynapticEdge\'s development incorporated, directly or derivatively, CMT\'s proprietary trade secret technology.

6\. These opinions are preliminary. I reserve the right to supplement, amend, or refine my opinions following review of additional discovery materials, including Veridian\'s source code for SynapticEdge, development documentation, version control history, internal communications, and depositions of relevant personnel.

**[IX. RESERVATION OF RIGHTS]{.underline}**

This preliminary report has been prepared in advance of the preliminary injunction hearing currently scheduled for October 15, 2024. As noted throughout this report, I have not yet had the opportunity to review Veridian\'s internal source code for the SynapticEdge platform, Veridian\'s design and development documents, version control logs, internal communications regarding SynapticEdge development, or other materials that are expected to be produced during the discovery period in this case. I understand that the discovery deadline in this matter is April 30, 2025, and that the trial is currently scheduled for September 8, 2025.

Upon review of such additional discovery materials, I anticipate providing a supplemental report with more detailed analysis, including code-level and design-level comparisons between SynapticEdge and NeuroGuide 4.0. Such detailed comparisons will be conducted under appropriate protective order provisions and will be presented in a manner consistent with the Court\'s orders regarding the handling of confidential information.

I reserve the right to supplement this report with additional analysis, opinions, or conclusions as warranted by additional materials I may review. I also reserve the right to respond to any expert report or rebuttal opinions submitted by or on behalf of the Defendants.

**[X. SIGNATURE]{.underline}**

I declare under penalty of perjury under the laws of the United States of America that the foregoing is true and correct to the best of my knowledge, information, and belief.

Respectfully submitted,

\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

Dr. Lena Yilmaz, Ph.D. Professor of Biomedical Engineering Kerrigan University

**[II. Background Facts]{.underline}**

The following summary provides the factual context underlying CMT\'s anticipated claims and the basis for the preservation demands set forth in this letter.

Ryan Oshiro served as Vice President of Engineering at CMT from March 2018 through March 1, 2024. During his tenure, Mr. Oshiro led the development of CMT\'s next-generation NeuroGuide 4.0 platform. **[III. Legal Obligation to Preserve]{.underline}**

It is well established that once a party reasonably anticipates litigation, it has a duty under the common law to preserve all documents, ESI, and tangible things that may be relevant to that litigation. This letter places Veridian on explicit and unambiguous notice that CMT anticipates filing litigation against Veridian and Mr. Oshiro, and that Veridian\'s obligation to preserve relevant evidence is now in effect.

Under Rule 37(e) of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure, if ESI that should have been preserved in the anticipation or conduct of litigation is lost because a party failed to take reasonable steps to preserve it, and the information cannot be restored or replaced through additional discovery, the Court may order measures no greater than necessary to cure the prejudice. Moreover, upon a finding that the party acted with the intent to deprive another party of the information\'s use in the litigation, the Court may presume that the lost information was unfavorable to the party, instruct the jury that it may or must presume the information was unfavorable, or dismiss the action or enter a default judgment. Courts routinely impose adverse inference instructions, monetary sanctions, and other remedial measures for spoliation of evidence.

This obligation to preserve extends to Veridian and all of its officers, directors, employees, agents, consultants, and any other persons acting on Veridian\'s behalf. result:
UNITED STATES DISTRICT COURT FOR THE WESTERN DISTRICT OF TEXAS AUSTIN DIVISION

**CASTILLO MEDICAL TECHNOLOGIES, INC.,** Plaintiff,

v\.

**RYAN OSHIRO** and **VERIDIAN HEALTH SYSTEMS, LLC,** Defendants.

**Case No. 1:24-cv-03841-RLH**

**ORDER GRANTING IN PART AND DENYING IN PART PLAINTIFF\'S MOTION FOR TEMPORARY RESTRAINING ORDER**

Before the Court is Plaintiff Castillo Medical Technologies, Inc.\'s (\"CMT\") Motion for Temporary Restraining Order. For the reasons stated below, the Motion is **GRANTED IN PART** and **DENIED IN PART**.

Hon. Rebecca L. Hightower United States District Judge

August 2, 2024

**[I. INTRODUCTION AND PROCEDURAL BACKGROUND]{.underline}**

On July 22, 2024, Plaintiff CMT filed its Verified Complaint in this action, asserting four causes of action against Defendants Ryan Oshiro (\"Oshiro\") and Veridian Health Systems, LLC (\"Veridian\"): (1) misappropriation of trade secrets under the Defend Trade Secrets Act (\"DTSA\"), 18 U.S.C. § 1836 *et seq.*; (2) misappropriation of trade secrets under the Texas Uniform Trade Secrets Act (\"TUTSA\"), Tex. Civ. Prac. & Rem. Code § 134A.001 *et seq.*; (3) breach of contract, specifically the Confidentiality, Non-Disclosure, and Invention Assignment Agreement executed by Oshiro; and (4) tortious interference with business relations. CMT is a Delaware corporation headquartered in Austin, Texas. Veridian is a Virginia limited liability company headquartered in Reston, Virginia. Oshiro is an individual residing in Alexandria, Virginia, and is a former Vice President of Engineering at CMT who now serves as Senior Vice President of Product Development at Veridian.

On July 25, 2024, CMT filed the instant Motion for Temporary Restraining Order, accompanied by supporting declarations and exhibits. Defendants filed their joint opposition on July 29, 2024. The Court held an expedited hearing on August 1, 2024, at which counsel for all parties appeared and presented argument. Margaret Hargrove and Kevin Nakamura of Hargrove, Pell & Sutton LLP appeared on behalf of Plaintiff CMT. Stephen Osei of Drummond & Wakefield LLP appeared on behalf of Defendant Veridian Health Systems, LLC. Patricia Dominguez of Barlow & Crane PLLC appeared on behalf of Defendant Oshiro. The Court has reviewed the motion, the supporting declarations and exhibits, Defendants\' opposition, the parties\' oral arguments at the August 1 hearing, and the applicable law, and is now prepared to rule.

**[II. FINDINGS OF FACT]{.underline}**

The Court makes the following preliminary findings of fact for purposes of this Motion only, without prejudice to either party\'s ability to present additional evidence at the preliminary injunction hearing. These findings are based on the record before the Court, including the verified complaint, the declarations and exhibits submitted by the parties, and the representations of counsel at the August 1, 2024 hearing. They do not constitute final findings on the merits.

**A. The Parties and Their Businesses**

CMT designs, manufactures, and licenses proprietary surgical robotic systems. Its flagship product is the \"NeuroGuide\" platform---an AI-assisted neurosurgical robot currently deployed in over 340 hospitals across North America. CMT\'s 2023 revenue was approximately \$287 million, and it employs approximately 1,100 people, the majority of whom work at its Austin, Texas headquarters. CMT holds 23 active United States patents and 14 pending patent applications related to real-time neural mapping and robotic arm micro-actuation, technologies central to the NeuroGuide platform and its competitive position in the surgical robotics market.

Veridian develops surgical robotics and AI-guided surgical planning software. In fiscal year 2023, Veridian reported revenue of approximately \$94 million and employed approximately 420 people. Veridian is headquartered in Reston, Virginia, with additional engineering offices in San Jose, California and Boston, Massachusetts. The parties do not dispute that CMT and Veridian are direct competitors in the surgical robotics market, competing for many of the same hospital and health-system customers.

**B. Oshiro\'s Employment at CMT and the NDA**

Oshiro served as Vice President of Engineering at CMT from March 2018 through March 1, 2024, a period of approximately six years. In that role, Oshiro reported directly to CMT\'s Chief Technology Officer, Dr. Priya Ramaswamy, and led the engineering team responsible for developing the next-generation \"NeuroGuide 4.0\" platform. Oshiro\'s position placed him at the center of CMT\'s most sensitive and strategically important research and development efforts.

On March 5, 2018, at the commencement of his employment, Oshiro executed a Confidentiality, Non-Disclosure, and Invention Assignment Agreement (the \"NDA\") with CMT. The NDA broadly defines \"Confidential Information\" to include, among other things, \"all technical data, algorithms, designs, processes, techniques, know-how, research, product plans, engineering specifications, software code, hardware schematics, and other proprietary information disclosed to, or learned by, Employee in connection with Employee\'s employment.\" The NDA imposes a 24-month post-employment non-disclosure period, which does not expire until March 1, 2026, and an 18-month non-solicitation clause, which does not expire until September 1, 2025. The Court specifically notes that the NDA does *not* contain a non-compete provision. This distinction is significant to the Court\'s analysis and will be discussed further below.

CMT invested approximately \$46.3 million in research and development for the NeuroGuide 4.0 platform between 2021 and 2023, with expenditures of approximately \$18.7 million in 2021, \$14.9 million in 2022, and \$12.7 million in 2023. Oshiro was the senior engineering leader overseeing the allocation and direction of these research and development resources throughout this period.

**C. The Trade Secrets at Issue**

CMT identifies three core categories of trade secrets that it alleges Defendants have misappropriated:

> \(1\) The **\"NeuralPath Algorithm\"**---a proprietary algorithm for real-time intraoperative neural mapping that enables the NeuroGuide system to generate a continuously updated map of neural structures during surgery, with sub-12-millisecond latency;
>
> \(2\) The **\"PrecisionDrive System\"**---a novel micro-actuation system enabling sub-millimeter robotic arm control using a proprietary harmonic drive configuration designed to minimize mechanical drift during neurosurgical procedures; and
>
> \(3\) The **\"PathPlanner Module\"**---a machine-learning-based surgical planning module that uses proprietary training datasets and model architectures to generate optimized surgical approach paths.

These three technologies constitute the core of the NeuroGuide 4.0 platform and represent the product of years of research, engineering effort, and substantial capital investment. CMT has made a sufficient preliminary showing that it took reasonable measures to protect the secrecy of these technologies. According to the declarations of Dr. Ramaswamy and CMT\'s Chief Information Security Officer, Gregory Tanaka, access to the relevant technical materials was restricted through CMT\'s secure engineering repository, known internally as \"VaultDrive,\" which employed role-based access controls, multi-factor authentication, and activity logging. CMT additionally required all employees with access to sensitive technical information to execute NDAs, and access to the three categories of trade secrets identified above was limited on a need-to-know basis within the engineering division.

**D. Oshiro\'s Departure and File Downloads**

Oshiro submitted his resignation letter on February 14, 2024. His last day of employment at CMT was March 1, 2024. In his resignation letter, Oshiro stated that he was \"pursuing personal interests and new professional opportunities\" but did not disclose his plans to join Veridian. On March 18, 2024---only 17 days after Oshiro\'s last day at CMT---Veridian publicly announced Oshiro\'s hiring as Senior Vice President of Product Development.

CMT\'s IT forensics investigation, conducted by Ashcroft Digital Forensics, LLC (\"Ashcroft\"), revealed that between January 8, 2024, and February 28, 2024---a period beginning approximately five weeks before Oshiro submitted his resignation---Oshiro downloaded approximately 4,700 files, totaling approximately 38.4 gigabytes, from CMT\'s VaultDrive to a personal external USB drive bearing serial number CX-8827491. According to the Ashcroft forensic report and the accompanying declaration of Ashcroft\'s principal, Jonathan Ashcroft, the downloaded files included:

> \(a\) Approximately 1,200 CAD schematics and associated engineering specifications for the PrecisionDrive System;
>
> \(b\) Approximately 2,100 source code files for the NeuralPath Algorithm, including proprietary libraries and configuration files;
>
> \(c\) Approximately 800 files comprising PathPlanner training datasets, model weights, and hyperparameter configurations; and
>
> \(d\) Approximately 600 internal documents, including R&D roadmap materials, competitive intelligence memoranda, and strategic planning documents related to the NeuroGuide 4.0 launch timeline.

The Ashcroft forensic report further indicates that on February 27, 2024---Oshiro\'s penultimate day of employment---Oshiro attempted to delete his VaultDrive access logs, though the deletion was captured by CMT\'s automated backup system and the logs were subsequently recovered. The timing of the downloads, beginning approximately five weeks before Oshiro\'s resignation and continuing until his penultimate day of employment, coupled with the attempted deletion of access logs, supports a reasonable inference of intentional exfiltration of CMT\'s proprietary data.

**E. Veridian\'s Recruitment of Oshiro and the SynapticEdge Announcement**

The evidence before the Court establishes the following timeline. On November 29, 2023, Veridian posted a job listing on LinkedIn for \"SVP, Product Development --- Surgical Robotics.\" CMT alleges, and has submitted evidence in the form of LinkedIn messaging records suggesting, that Veridian\'s Chief Executive Officer, Marcus Trent, personally contacted Oshiro via LinkedIn on December 3, 2023---more than two months before Oshiro submitted his resignation from CMT. Oshiro\'s compensation at Veridian---a \$425,000 base salary, a \$200,000 signing bonus, and equity grants valued at approximately \$750,000 vesting over four years---represents a substantial premium over his CMT compensation package, which consisted of a \$310,000 base salary and equity grants valued at approximately \$180,000 per year.

On June 11, 2024, Veridian announced its \"SynapticEdge\" surgical robotics platform at the International Surgical Robotics Conference in Chicago, Illinois. This announcement came only approximately 85 days---roughly two and a half months---after Oshiro commenced employment at Veridian on or about March 18, 2024. The compressed timeline between Oshiro\'s hiring and the public debut of SynapticEdge raises significant questions about the origins and development history of the SynapticEdge platform. The SynapticEdge press release specifically credited Oshiro as \"leading the development effort\" behind the platform, and Veridian\'s CEO Marcus Trent was quoted in the announcement as stating that Oshiro\'s \"vision and expertise have been instrumental in bringing SynapticEdge to life.\"

CMT\'s expert, Dr. Lena Yilmaz, Professor of Biomedical Engineering at Kerrigan University and a recognized authority in surgical robotics systems, submitted a preliminary declaration identifying at least five specific technical features of SynapticEdge that appear to mirror the proprietary architecture of CMT\'s NeuroGuide 4.0 platform:

> \(1\) A real-time neural mapping overlay with sub-12-millisecond latency specifications that are nearly identical to the NeuralPath Algorithm\'s performance parameters;
>
> \(2\) A micro-actuation approach employing a harmonic drive configuration substantially similar to CMT\'s PrecisionDrive System;
>
> \(3\) A machine-learning surgical planning module that appears to have been trained on a dataset structure closely resembling the PathPlanner Module\'s proprietary training data architecture;
>
> \(4\) An identical user-interface workflow for surgeon calibration, including the same sequence of calibration steps and visual feedback mechanisms used in NeuroGuide 4.0; and
>
> \(5\) A shared error-correction protocol for robotic arm drift that utilizes the same mathematical approach to compensating for mechanical imprecision during procedures.

Dr. Yilmaz opines in her declaration that the probability of independent parallel development of all five of these features---each of which reflects specific, non-obvious design choices---is \"extraordinarily low.\" She estimates that developing a surgical robotics platform incorporating features of this complexity and specificity would ordinarily require a minimum of two to three years of dedicated research and engineering effort by a team of experienced professionals, even assuming adequate funding and staffing.

The Court finds the compressed development timeline particularly troubling. A platform incorporating five complex, advanced technical features of the kind described by Dr. Yilmaz would ordinarily require years of development. Veridian has not, at this preliminary stage, offered a credible explanation for how SynapticEdge could have been developed to its announced state of readiness in the approximately 85-day period following Oshiro\'s arrival. While Veridian\'s counsel represented at the hearing that SynapticEdge was \"in development prior to Mr. Oshiro\'s hiring,\" Veridian did not submit documentary evidence---such as development logs, project management records, commit histories, or engineering milestone reports---to substantiate that contention. This omission is notable. The extent of the technical overlap identified by Dr. Yilmaz, combined with the forensic evidence of substantial data exfiltration by Oshiro, supports a strong inference that CMT\'s trade secrets were used in SynapticEdge\'s development.

**F. Departure of Additional CMT Engineers**

Since Oshiro joined Veridian, three additional CMT engineers who previously reported to Oshiro in CMT\'s NeuroGuide 4.0 engineering division have departed CMT and joined Veridian in close succession: Dr. Kenji Furukawa, a senior robotics engineer, resigned from CMT on April 22, 2024, and joined Veridian on May 13, 2024; Samantha Briggs, a software architect, resigned from CMT on May 8, 2024, and joined Veridian on June 3, 2024; and David Moreno, a mechanical systems engineer, resigned from CMT on June 18, 2024, and joined Veridian on July 8, 2024. All three now report to Oshiro at Veridian. The Court notes the pattern of departures but observes that CMT\'s solicitation claims are not the primary basis for the instant TRO motion. The circumstances surrounding these departures will be further explored at the preliminary injunction stage, and the Court makes no findings at this time regarding whether these departures resulted from improper solicitation by Oshiro or Veridian.

**[III. LEGAL STANDARD]{.underline}**

A temporary restraining order is an extraordinary remedy that should not be granted unless the movant clearly establishes its entitlement to relief. *See Holland Am. Ins. Co. v. Succession of Roy*, 777 F.2d 992, 997 (5th Cir. 1985). To obtain a TRO, the movant must demonstrate: (1) a substantial likelihood of success on the merits; (2) a substantial threat of irreparable injury if the restraining order is not granted; (3) the threatened injury to the movant outweighs any potential harm to the party to be restrained; and (4) granting the restraining order will not disserve the public interest. *See Janvey v. Alguire*, 647 F.3d 585, 595 (5th Cir. 2011); *Nichols v. Alcatel USA, Inc.*, 532 F.3d 364, 372 (5th Cir. 2008). The movant bears the burden of establishing each of these four elements. *See Speaks v. Kruse*, 445 F.3d 396, 399--400 (5th Cir. 2006).

The DTSA specifically provides for injunctive relief to prevent actual or threatened misappropriation of trade secrets, provided that such relief does not \"prevent a person from entering into an employment relationship\" and that \"conditions placed on such employment shall be based on evidence of threatened misappropriation and not merely on the information the person knows.\" 18 U.S.C. § 1836(b)(3)(A). This statutory limitation reinforces the importance of carefully tailoring any injunctive relief in trade secret cases involving former employees.

**[IV. ANALYSIS]{.underline}**

**A. Likelihood of Success on the Merits**

**Trade Secret Misappropriation (DTSA and TUTSA).** CMT has demonstrated a substantial likelihood of success on the merits of its trade secret misappropriation claims under both the DTSA and TUTSA. To prevail on such claims, CMT must show that the information at issue qualifies as a trade secret and that it was misappropriated. *See 18 U.S.C. § 1839(3), (5); Tex. Civ. Prac. & Rem. Code § 134A.002(3), (6).* The three technologies identified by CMT---the NeuralPath Algorithm, the PrecisionDrive System, and the PathPlanner Module---qualify as trade secrets at this preliminary stage. They derive independent economic value from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable through proper means by, other persons who can obtain economic value from their disclosure or use. CMT has further demonstrated that it took reasonable measures to maintain their secrecy, including the use of the VaultDrive secure repository, role-based access controls, NDA requirements, and need-to-know access limitations.

The forensic evidence of Oshiro\'s download of approximately 4,700 files---totaling 38.4 gigabytes of proprietary data---to personal USB drive CX-8827491 in the weeks preceding his departure, coupled with his attempted deletion of access logs, supports a strong inference of misappropriation by improper means. The SynapticEdge development timeline is a significant additional factor. The Court finds it difficult to credit, at this preliminary stage, that Veridian independently developed a platform with five features closely paralleling CMT\'s proprietary NeuroGuide 4.0 architecture in the approximately 85 days between Oshiro\'s start date at Veridian and the SynapticEdge announcement on June 11, 2024. While Veridian contends that SynapticEdge was in development prior to Oshiro\'s arrival, the extent of the technical overlap identified by Dr. Yilmaz---combined with the forensic evidence of data exfiltration---supports an inference that CMT\'s trade secrets were used in SynapticEdge\'s development. Veridian will have the opportunity to present a fuller account of SynapticEdge\'s development history at the preliminary injunction hearing, and the Court emphasizes that its findings at this stage are preliminary.

**Breach of NDA.** CMT has also shown a substantial likelihood of success on its breach of contract claim. Oshiro\'s NDA contains a 24-month non-disclosure obligation that remains in effect until March 1, 2026. The evidence before the Court---including the forensic evidence of the file downloads, the compressed SynapticEdge development timeline, and the technical overlap between SynapticEdge and NeuroGuide 4.0---supports an inference that Oshiro disclosed CMT\'s confidential information to Veridian or used it in his work at Veridian, in violation of his contractual non-disclosure obligations.

**Tortious Interference.** CMT has demonstrated some likelihood of success on its tortious interference claim, but it has not yet made a full showing on all elements. In particular, the claim requires further factual development regarding the specific business relationships at issue, the nature and extent of Veridian\'s knowledge of those relationships, and the allegedly improper conduct. The Court will revisit this claim at the preliminary injunction stage.

**B. Irreparable Injury**

CMT has demonstrated a substantial threat of irreparable injury if a restraining order is not granted. Trade secret misappropriation, by its nature, causes irreparable harm because once trade secrets are disclosed, they cannot be \"unlearned\" or restored to their former state of secrecy. *See Tex. Advanced Optoelectronic Sols., Inc. v. Renesas Elecs. Am., Inc.*, 895 F.3d 1304, 1315 (Fed. Cir. 2018) (applying Fifth Circuit law); *FMC Corp. v. Taiwan Taiyo Yuden Co.*, 743 F.2d 1470, 1473 (Fed. Cir. 1984).

CMT invested \$46.3 million in the development of the NeuroGuide 4.0 platform. Continued marketing or demonstration of SynapticEdge\'s neural mapping feature risks further dissemination of CMT\'s proprietary technology to potential customers, industry participants, and the public at large. While CMT alleges approximately \$82 million in actual damages, money damages alone may be inadequate because the competitive harm resulting from the loss of trade secret status is inherently difficult to quantify with precision. If Veridian is permitted to continue marketing SynapticEdge\'s neural mapping feature pending full adjudication, and that feature is ultimately found to derive from CMT\'s NeuralPath Algorithm, the trade secret may be irretrievably destroyed. This concern is particularly acute given that SynapticEdge was demonstrated to attendees at the International Surgical Robotics Conference in Chicago on June 11, 2024, an event attended by hundreds of industry participants and potential customers.

**C. Balance of Hardships**

The Court must weigh the harm to CMT if the TRO is denied against the harm to Defendants if it is granted. If the TRO is denied, CMT faces the potential permanent loss of trade secret status for the NeuralPath Algorithm, continued and compounding competitive injury, and further dissemination of its proprietary technology into the marketplace. These harms could fundamentally and irreversibly alter the competitive landscape in which CMT operates.

Conversely, if the TRO is granted broadly---for example, by enjoining all SynapticEdge activities or prohibiting Oshiro\'s employment at Veridian entirely---the harm to Defendants would be significant. Veridian employs approximately 420 people, received a \$60 million Series C investment from Thorncastle Ventures, and has legitimate business interests in its ongoing operations. A total injunction of SynapticEdge or of Oshiro\'s employment would cause substantial disruption to Veridian\'s business.

The Court will therefore narrowly tailor the TRO to minimize harm to Defendants while protecting CMT\'s rights. Specifically, the Court will enjoin only the marketing, licensing, and demonstration of SynapticEdge\'s neural mapping feature---the feature most directly linked to the NeuralPath Algorithm trade secret as established by the current evidentiary record and Dr. Yilmaz\'s analysis---rather than all SynapticEdge activities. The Court declines to enjoin Oshiro from working at Veridian entirely. The NDA does not contain a non-compete clause, and CMT has not demonstrated that a total employment injunction is warranted at this stage. The Court will not effectively impose a non-compete through injunctive relief absent a contractual basis for doing so. *See 18 U.S.C. § 1836(b)(3)(A)(i)(I)* (injunctive relief under DTSA shall not \"prevent a person from entering into an employment relationship\"). The absence of a non-compete is significant, and the Court\'s Order is crafted to respect that contractual reality.

**D. Public Interest**

The public interest favors protection of trade secrets and enforcement of contractual obligations. The protection of intellectual property incentivizes the investment and innovation that drives advances in surgical robotics---a field with direct and substantial implications for public health. At the same time, the public has a strong interest in promoting competition and employee mobility. The narrow tailoring of this Order serves both interests. By targeting only the specific feature most closely linked to the alleged misappropriation, the Court preserves both the integrity of CMT\'s trade secrets and Veridian\'s ability to compete in the surgical robotics market through its own lawful innovations. Innovation in surgical robotics ultimately serves patients and the medical community, and the Court\'s Order is designed to protect legitimate intellectual property without unduly stifling competition.

**[V. ORDER]{.underline}**

For the foregoing reasons, IT IS HEREBY ORDERED as follows:

**A. TRO Granted in Part --- Injunction Against Marketing SynapticEdge Neural Mapping Feature**

Defendant Veridian Health Systems, LLC, and its officers, agents, servants, employees, and attorneys, and all persons in active concert or participation with them who receive actual notice of this Order by personal service or otherwise, are **TEMPORARILY RESTRAINED AND ENJOINED** from:

> \(a\) Marketing, licensing, selling, offering for sale, or publicly demonstrating the neural mapping feature (or any substantially similar feature) of the SynapticEdge surgical robotics platform to any person or entity;
>
> \(b\) Disclosing to any third party the technical specifications, architecture, source code, or functionality of the SynapticEdge neural mapping feature, whether through written materials, oral presentations, product demonstrations, or any other means; and
>
> \(c\) Providing access to the SynapticEdge neural mapping feature to any hospital, medical institution, healthcare system, or other potential customer, whether through trial deployments, beta testing, licensing agreements, or any other arrangement.

This injunction is limited to the neural mapping feature and does not extend to other features or aspects of the SynapticEdge platform that CMT has not shown, at this stage, to be derived from its trade secrets. The Court\'s injunction is specifically tied to the neural mapping feature because the evidentiary record---including the SynapticEdge development timeline and Dr. Yilmaz\'s expert analysis---most strongly supports misappropriation of the NeuralPath Algorithm with respect to this feature.

**B. Document Preservation Requirement**

Defendant Veridian Health Systems, LLC is **ORDERED** to preserve all documents, electronically stored information (\"ESI\"), and tangible items related to:

> \(a\) The development of the SynapticEdge platform, including but not limited to design documents, source code, source code repository commit logs, CAD files, meeting notes, project management records (including Jira, Confluence, or similar tools), Slack or other messaging platform communications, and email correspondence;
>
> \(b\) The recruitment and hiring of Ryan Oshiro, including all communications between any Veridian employee, officer, director, or agent and Oshiro prior to and following his hiring;
>
> \(c\) Any materials, files, or data brought by Oshiro to Veridian from CMT, including but not limited to any data from USB drive bearing serial number CX-8827491 or any copies, derivatives, or reproductions thereof;
>
> \(d\) The recruitment or hiring of Dr. Kenji Furukawa, Samantha Briggs, and David Moreno, including all communications relating to their departure from CMT and hiring at Veridian; and
>
> \(e\) Any internal assessments, reports, analyses, or evaluations of CMT\'s NeuroGuide platform, NeuroGuide 4.0, or CMT\'s technology, products, or competitive position.

Veridian shall implement a litigation hold, if it has not already done so, within forty-eight (48) hours of entry of this Order, and shall confirm compliance in writing to Plaintiff\'s counsel within that same period. Failure to comply with this preservation requirement may result in sanctions under Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 37(e), including adverse inference instructions, monetary sanctions, or other relief the Court deems appropriate.

**C. TRO Denied in Part --- CMT\'s Request to Enjoin Oshiro\'s Employment**

CMT\'s request to enjoin Defendant Ryan Oshiro from working at Veridian Health Systems, LLC in any capacity is **DENIED**. The NDA between Oshiro and CMT does not contain a non-compete provision. While the NDA imposes non-disclosure and non-solicitation obligations that remain in effect, it does not prohibit Oshiro from accepting employment with a competitor. The Court will not effectively impose a non-compete through injunctive relief absent a contractual basis for doing so. The DTSA itself provides that injunctive relief shall not \"prevent a person from entering into an employment relationship,\" and the Court is guided by that statutory directive. *See* 18 U.S.C. § 1836(b)(3)(A)(i)(I).

CMT remains free to pursue its claims based on breach of the NDA\'s non-disclosure and non-solicitation provisions, and Oshiro remains bound by those contractual obligations notwithstanding his employment at Veridian. This denial is without prejudice to CMT\'s ability to renew this request at the preliminary injunction hearing if additional evidence warrants a different conclusion.

**D. Preliminary Injunction Hearing**

A hearing on Plaintiff\'s Motion for Preliminary Injunction is hereby **SET** for **October 15, 2024, at 9:00 a.m.** in Courtroom 3 of the United States Courthouse, 501 West 5th Street, Austin, Texas 78701.

The parties shall confer and submit a joint proposed briefing schedule for the preliminary injunction motion no later than **August 16, 2024**. The Court expects the parties to address the scope of any expedited discovery needed in advance of the preliminary injunction hearing.

The Court specifically directs that Veridian address at the preliminary injunction hearing the development history and timeline of the SynapticEdge platform, including: (i) when development of SynapticEdge commenced; (ii) the identity of all personnel involved in SynapticEdge\'s development, both before and after Oshiro\'s arrival; (iii) documentary evidence---such as source code repository logs, project management records, engineering milestone reports, and internal communications---supporting Veridian\'s contention, if any, that SynapticEdge was developed independently of CMT\'s trade secrets; and (iv) the specific contributions, if any, made by Oshiro to the SynapticEdge platform since his hiring. This information is critical to the Court\'s evaluation of the merits at the preliminary injunction stage.

**E. Duration and Bond**

This Temporary Restraining Order shall remain in effect for **fourteen (14) days** from the date of entry, *i.e.*, until **August 16, 2024**, unless extended by the Court for good cause shown pursuant to Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 65(b)(2).

Pursuant to Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 65(c), Plaintiff Castillo Medical Technologies, Inc. shall post a bond in the amount of **\$500,000.00** within five (5) business days of this Order as security for the payment of such costs and damages as may be incurred or suffered by Defendants if they are found to have been wrongfully enjoined or restrained. The Court has considered the parties\' arguments regarding the appropriate bond amount and finds \$500,000.00 to be adequate and reasonable in light of the narrow scope of the TRO---which is limited to the neural mapping feature of SynapticEdge---and the significant harm to CMT\'s trade secrets that this Order is designed to prevent.

**[VI. CONCLUSION]{.underline}**

For the reasons set forth above, Plaintiff Castillo Medical Technologies, Inc.\'s Motion for Temporary Restraining Order is **GRANTED IN PART** and **DENIED IN PART** as follows:

> 1\. The Motion is **GRANTED** to the extent it seeks to temporarily restrain Veridian from marketing, licensing, selling, demonstrating, or disclosing the neural mapping feature of the SynapticEdge platform, as set forth in Section V.A above.
>
> 2\. The Motion is **GRANTED** to the extent it seeks a document preservation order, as set forth in Section V.B above.
>
> 3\. The Motion is **DENIED** to the extent it seeks to enjoin Defendant Oshiro from working at Veridian, as set forth in Section V.C above.

The Clerk of Court is directed to serve this Order on all parties forthwith.

**IT IS SO ORDERED.**

\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

Hon. Rebecca L. Hightower United States District Judge Western District of Texas

**INTERROGATORY NO. 2.** Identify and describe in detail each Communication between any Veridian Person and Oshiro from November 1, 2023 through March 18, 2024, including the LinkedIn job posting for "SVP, Product Development — Surgical Robotics" dated on or about November 29, 2023 and the LinkedIn message sent by Marcus Trent to Oshiro on or about December 3, 2023, and for each such Communication state: (a) the date; (b) the participants; (c) the form; and (d) the substance or content.

**INTERROGATORY NO. 4.** State all facts concerning Veridian's knowledge, at any time before or after hiring Oshiro, of Oshiro's obligations to CMT under the NDA, including: (a) whether Veridian inquired whether Oshiro was subject to any confidentiality, non-disclosure, non-solicitation, or invention-assignment obligations to CMT or any prior employer; (b) the identity of all Persons who made or received any such inquiry; (c) any steps Veridian took to ensure Oshiro would not breach any obligations to CMT; and (d) the identity of all Documents concerning any such inquiry or steps.

**INTERROGATORY NO. 6.** State all facts concerning whether Oshiro brought, transferred, uploaded, or made available to Veridian or any Veridian Person any CMT files, data, materials, or CMT Confidential Information, including any files originating from VaultDrive or contained on the USB Drive, and identify each such file or category of files and the date(s) on which it was brought, transferred, or made available.

**INTERROGATORY NO. 7.** State all facts concerning whether the USB Drive (or any copy, clone, image, or derivative thereof) has ever been connected to, accessed by, or present on any Veridian computer, server, workstation, mobile device, or network, and for each such instance state: (a) the date; (b) the device or system involved; (c) the Person who connected or accessed it; and (d) what was done with any data thereon.

**INTERROGATORY NO. 9.** State all facts concerning any information barriers, ethical walls, clean-room procedures, or other protocols Veridian implemented (or considered implementing) to prevent the use or disclosure of CMT's proprietary information following the hiring of Oshiro or any of the Three Engineers, and identify all Documents concerning any such measures, including any Documents reflecting a decision not to implement any such measures.
